The **Metropolitan Museum of Art**, colloquially referred to as **the Met**, is an [encyclopedic](/wiki/Encyclopedic_museum "Encyclopedic museum") [art museum](/wiki/Art_museum "Art museum") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). By floor area, it is the [fourth-largest museum in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_museums "List of largest museums") and the [largest art museum](/wiki/List_of_largest_art_museums "List of largest art museums") in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). With 5,727,258 visitors in fiscal year 2025, it was the [most-visited museum in the United States](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_museums_in_the_United_States "List of most-visited museums in the United States") and the [fourth-most visited art museum in the world](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_art_museums "List of most-visited art museums").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 2000, its permanent collection had over two million works;[[1]](#cite_note-www.metmuseum.org-1-1) it currently lists a total of 1.5 million works.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The collection is divided into 17 curatorial departments. [The main building](/wiki/The_Met_Fifth_Avenue "The Met Fifth Avenue") at 1000 [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue"), along the [Museum Mile](/wiki/Museum_Mile,_New_York "Museum Mile, New York") on the eastern edge of [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") on [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")'s [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side"), is by area one of the world's [largest art museums](/wiki/List_of_largest_art_museums "List of largest art museums"). The first portion of the approximately 2-million-square-foot (190,000 m2) building was built in 1880. A much smaller second location, [The Cloisters](/wiki/The_Cloisters "The Cloisters") at [Fort Tryon Park](/wiki/Fort_Tryon_Park "Fort Tryon Park") in [Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan"), contains an extensive collection of [art](/wiki/Art "Art"), [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture"), and [artifacts](/wiki/Artifact_(archaeology) "Artifact (archaeology)") from [medieval Europe](/wiki/Medieval_Europe "Medieval Europe").

The Metropolitan Museum of Art was founded in 1870. The museum was established by a group of Americans, including philanthropists, artists, and businessmen, with the goal of creating a national institution that would inspire and educate the public.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The museum's permanent collection consists of works of art ranging from the [ancient Near East](/wiki/Ancient_Near_East "Ancient Near East") and [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Art_of_ancient_Egypt "Art of ancient Egypt"), through [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") to the contemporary world. It includes [paintings](/wiki/Painting "Painting"), [sculptures](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture"), and [graphic](/wiki/Graphics "Graphics") works from many [European](/wiki/Western_painting "Western painting") [Old Masters](/wiki/Old_Master "Old Master"), as well as an extensive collection of [American](/wiki/Visual_art_of_the_United_States "Visual art of the United States"), [modern, and contemporary art](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art"). The Met also maintains extensive holdings of [African](/wiki/African_art "African art"), [Asian](/wiki/Asian_art "Asian art"), [Oceanian](/wiki/Oceanian_art "Oceanian art"), [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_art "Byzantine art"), and [Islamic art](/wiki/Islamic_art "Islamic art"). The museum is home to encyclopedic collections of [musical instruments](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument"), [costumes](/wiki/Costume "Costume"), and [decorative arts](/wiki/Decorative_arts "Decorative arts") and [textiles](/wiki/Textile "Textile"), as well as antique [weapons](/wiki/Weapon "Weapon") and [armor](/wiki/Armor "Armor") from around the world. Several notable interiors, ranging from 1st-century [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") through modern American design, are installed in its galleries.

## Collections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Collections")]

The Met's permanent collection is curated by seventeen separate departments, each with a specialized staff of [curators](/wiki/Curator "Curator") and scholars, as well as six dedicated conservation departments and a Department of Scientific Research.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The permanent collection includes works of art from [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") and [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Art_of_ancient_Egypt "Art of ancient Egypt"); paintings and sculptures from nearly all the [European masters](/wiki/Western_painting "Western painting"); and an extensive collection of [American](/wiki/Visual_art_of_the_United_States "Visual art of the United States") and [modern art](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art"). The Met maintains extensive holdings of [African](/wiki/African_art "African art"), [Asian](/wiki/Asian_art "Asian art"), [Oceanian](/wiki/Oceanian_art "Oceanian art"), [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_art "Byzantine art"), and [Islamic art](/wiki/Islamic_art "Islamic art").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The museum is also home to encyclopedic collections of [musical instruments](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument"), [costumes](/wiki/Costume "Costume") and accessories, and antique [weapons](/wiki/Weapon "Weapon") and [armor](/wiki/Armor "Armor") from around the world.[[12]](#cite_note-12) A great number of [period rooms](/wiki/Period_room "Period room"), ranging from [first-century Rome](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") through modern American design, are permanently installed in the Met's galleries.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Since the late 1800s, the Museum has been collecting diverse materials from all over the world. Its outreach to "exhibition designers, architects, graphic designers, lighting designers, and production designers" helps the museum to maintain its collection in good condition.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Geographically designated collections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geographically designated collections")]

#### Ancient Near Eastern art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ancient Near Eastern art")]

Beginning in the late 19th century, the Met started acquiring ancient art and artifacts from the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East"). From a few [cuneiform](/wiki/Cuneiform "Cuneiform") tablets and [seals](/wiki/Seal_(emblem) "Seal (emblem)"), the museum's collection of Near Eastern art has grown to more than 7,000 pieces.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Representing a history of the region beginning in the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") Period and encompassing the fall of the [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire") and the end of [Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity"), the collection includes works from the [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer"), [Hittite](/wiki/Hittites "Hittites"), Sasanian, [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyria "Assyria"), [Babylonian](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia"), and [Elamite](/wiki/Elam "Elam") cultures (among others), as well as an extensive collection of unique [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") objects. The highlights of the collection include the Sumerian [Stele of Ushumgal](/wiki/Stele_of_Ushumgal "Stele of Ushumgal"), the Elamite silver [Kneeling Bull with Vessel](/wiki/Kneeling_Bull_with_Vessel "Kneeling Bull with Vessel"), the [Pratt Ivories](/wiki/Pratt_Ivories "Pratt Ivories"), and a set of monumental stone *[lamassu](/wiki/Lamassu "Lamassu")*, or guardian figures, from the Northwest Palace of the Assyrian king [Ashurnasirpal II](/wiki/Ashurnasirpal_II "Ashurnasirpal II").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

#### Arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Queen_Mother_Pendant_Mask-_Iyoba_MET_DP231460.jpg/250px-Queen_Mother_Pendant_Mask-_Iyoba_MET_DP231460.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Mother_Pendant_Mask-_Iyoba_MET_DP231460.jpg)

[Benin ivory mask](/wiki/Benin_ivory_mask "Benin ivory mask"), Iyoba, 16th-century Nigeria

Though the Met first acquired a group of [Peruvian](/wiki/Peruvians "Peruvians") antiquities in 1882, in addition to Mesoamerican antiquities, the museum did not begin a concerted effort to collect works from [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania"), and the Americas until 1969, when American businessman, [philanthropist](/wiki/Philanthropist "Philanthropist") and then NY Gov. [Nelson A. Rockefeller](/wiki/Nelson_Rockefeller "Nelson Rockefeller") donated his more than 3,000-piece collection to the museum. Before Rockefeller's collection was gifted to the Met, Rockefeller founded [The Museum of Primitive Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Primitive_Art "Museum of Primitive Art") in New York City with the intention of displaying these works, after the Met had previously shown little interest in his art collection.[[17]](#cite_note-LaGamma-17) In 1968, the Met had agreed to a temporary exhibition of Rockefeller's work. However, the Met then requested to include the arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas in their permanent collection.[[17]](#cite_note-LaGamma-17) The arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas opened to the public in 1982, under the title, "The Michael C. Rockefeller Wing".[[18]](#cite_note-Observer-18) This wing is named after Nelson Rockefeller's son, [Michael Rockefeller](/wiki/Michael_Rockefeller "Michael Rockefeller"), who died while collecting works in [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Today, the Met's collection contains more than 11,000 pieces from [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"), the [Pacific Islands](/wiki/Pacific_Islands "Pacific Islands"), and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") and is housed in the 40,000-square-foot (4,000 m2) Rockefeller Wing on the south end of the museum.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The Wing exhibits Non-Western works of art created from 3,000 [BCE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era") – present, including a wide range of particular cultural traditions.[[18]](#cite_note-Observer-18) Significantly, this work was regarded as art, judged on aesthetic terms, in a Western art museum. Before then, objects from Africa, Oceania, and the Americas were often considered to be the work of "primitives" or [ethnographic](/wiki/Ethnography "Ethnography") work, rather than art.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The Wing exhibits the arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas in an exhibition separated by geographical locations. The collection ranges from 40,000-year-old [indigenous Australian rock paintings](/wiki/Aboriginal_rock_art "Aboriginal rock art"), to a group of 15-foot-tall (4.6 m) memorial poles carved by the [Asmat people](/wiki/Asmat_people "Asmat people") of [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea"), to a priceless collection of ceremonial and personal objects from the [Nigerian](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") [Court of Benin](/wiki/Benin_Empire "Benin Empire") donated by [Klaus Perls](/wiki/Klaus_Perls "Klaus Perls").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Curator of African Art [Susan Mullin Vogel](/wiki/Susan_Mullin_Vogel "Susan Mullin Vogel") discussed a famous [Benin](/wiki/Benin "Benin") artifact acquired by the Metropolitan Museum of Art in 1972. It was originally auctioned in April 1900 by a lieutenant named [Augustus Pitt Rivers](/wiki/Augustus_Pitt_Rivers "Augustus Pitt Rivers") at the price of 37 [guineas](/wiki/Guinea_(coin) "Guinea (coin)").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In December 2021, the Met began its $70 million (~$79.7 million in 2024) renovation of The Michael C. Rockefeller Wing's African, ancient American, and Oceanic art galleries, originally planned to begin in 2020 but now set for completion in 2024. The 40,000 square-feet renovation includes the reinstallation of an exterior glass curtain, which had deteriorated, as well as the galleries in their entirety, which house 3,000 works.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

#### Asian art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Asian art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/NatarajaMET.JPG/250px-NatarajaMET.JPG)](/wiki/File:NatarajaMET.JPG)

[Bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") [Chola](/wiki/Chola "Chola") Statue of *[Nataraja](/wiki/Nataraja "Nataraja")*, 11th century, [Tamilnadu](/wiki/Tamilnadu "Tamilnadu")

The Met's Asian department holds a collection of Asian art, of more than 35,000 pieces,[[25]](#cite_note-25) that is arguably the most comprehensive in the US. The collection dates back almost to the founding of the museum: many of the philanthropists who made the earliest gifts to the museum included Asian art in their collections. Today, an entire wing of the museum is dedicated to the Asian collection, and spans 4,000 years of Asian art. Major Asian civilizations are well-represented in the Met's Asian department. The pieces on display represent diverse types of [decorative art](/wiki/Decorative_arts "Decorative arts"), from painting and [printmaking](/wiki/Printmaking "Printmaking") to [sculpture](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture") and [metalworking](/wiki/Metalworking "Metalworking"). The department is well known for its comprehensive collection of [Cambodian](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), [Indian](/wiki/Sculpture_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Sculpture in the Indian subcontinent"), and [Chinese art](/wiki/Chinese_art "Chinese art") (including [calligraphy](/wiki/Chinese_calligraphy "Chinese calligraphy") and [painting](/wiki/Chinese_painting "Chinese painting")), as well as for its [Nepalese](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") and [Tibetan](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") works, and the arts of [Burma](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") (Myanmar), and [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). Three ancient religions of India—[Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism")—are well represented in these sculptures.[[26]](#cite_note-26) However, not only "art" and ritual objects are represented in the collection; many of the best-known pieces are functional objects. The Asian wing also contains the [Astor Court](/wiki/Astor_Court_(Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art) "Astor Court (Metropolitan Museum of Art)"), a complete [Ming Dynasty](/wiki/Ming_Dynasty "Ming Dynasty")-style [garden court](/wiki/Astor_Court "Astor Court"), modeled on a courtyard in the [Master of the Nets Garden](/wiki/Master_of_the_Nets_Garden "Master of the Nets Garden") in [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou,_Jiangsu "Suzhou, Jiangsu"). Maxwell K. Hearn has been the current department chairman of Asian Art since 2011.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

#### Egyptian art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Egyptian art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Standing_Hippopotamus_MET_DP248993.jpg/250px-Standing_Hippopotamus_MET_DP248993.jpg)](/wiki/File:Standing_Hippopotamus_MET_DP248993.jpg)

[William the Hippopotamus](/wiki/William_the_Faience_Hippopotamus "William the Faience Hippopotamus") is a mascot of the Met.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Mummy%2C_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_NYC.jpg/250px-Mummy%2C_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_NYC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mummy,_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_NYC.jpg)

Mummy, Metropolitan Museum of Art

Though the majority of the Met's initial holdings of [Egyptian](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") art came from private collections, items uncovered during the museum's own archeological excavations, carried out between 1906 and 1941, constitute almost half of the current collection. More than 26,000 separate pieces of Egyptian art from the [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") era through the [Ptolemaic](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom") era constitute the Met's Egyptian collection, and almost all of them are on display in the museum's massive wing of 40 Egyptian galleries.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Among the rarest pieces in the Met's Egyptian collection are 13 wooden models (of the total 24 models found together, 12 models and 1 offering bearer figure is at the Met, while the remaining 10 models and 1 offering bearer figure are in the [Egyptian Museum](/wiki/Egyptian_Museum "Egyptian Museum") in [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo")), discovered in a tomb in the Southern Asasif in western [Thebes](/wiki/Thebes,_Egypt "Thebes, Egypt") in 1920. These models depict, in unparalleled detail, a cross-section of Egyptian life in the early [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"): boats, gardens, and scenes of daily life are represented in [miniature](/wiki/Miniature_art "Miniature art"). [William the Faience Hippopotamus](/wiki/William_the_Faience_Hippopotamus "William the Faience Hippopotamus") is a miniature that has become the informal mascot of the museum. Other notable items in the Egyptian collection include the [Chair of Reniseneb](/wiki/Chair_of_Reniseneb "Chair of Reniseneb"), the [Lotiform Chalice](/wiki/Lotiform_vessels_(Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art) "Lotiform vessels (Metropolitan Museum of Art)"), and the [Metternich Stela](/wiki/Metternich_Stela "Metternich Stela").

However, the popular centerpiece of the Egyptian Art department continues to be the [Temple of Dendur](/wiki/Temple_of_Dendur "Temple of Dendur"). Dismantled by the Egyptian government as part of the [International Campaign to Save the Monuments of Nubia](/wiki/International_Campaign_to_Save_the_Monuments_of_Nubia "International Campaign to Save the Monuments of Nubia") to save it from rising waters caused by the building of the [Aswan High Dam](/wiki/Aswan_High_Dam "Aswan High Dam"), the large [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") temple was given to the United States in 1965 and assembled in a new wing at the Met in 1978. Situated in a large room and partially surrounded by a reflecting pool and illuminated by a wall of windows opening onto Central Park, the Temple of Dendur has been one of the Met's most enduring attractions. Among the oldest items at the Met, a set of Archeulian flints from [Deir el-Bahri](/wiki/Deir_el-Bahri "Deir el-Bahri") which date from the [Lower Paleolithic](/wiki/Lower_Paleolithic "Lower Paleolithic") period (between 300,000 and 75,000 BCE), are part of the Egyptian collection. The first curator was [Albert Lythgoe](/wiki/Albert_Lythgoe "Albert Lythgoe"), who directed several Egyptian excavations for the museum.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Since 2013 the curator has been Diana Craig Patch.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 2018, the museum built an exhibition around the golden-sheathed 1st-century BCE [coffin of Nedjemankh](/wiki/Coffin_of_Nedjemankh "Coffin of Nedjemankh"), a high-ranking priest of the ram-headed god [Heryshaf](/wiki/Heryshaf "Heryshaf") of [Heracleopolis](/wiki/Heracleopolis_Magna "Heracleopolis Magna"). Investigators determined that the artifact had been stolen in 2011 from Egypt, and the museum returned it.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

#### European paintings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: European paintings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/European_paintings_at_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_%28NYC%2C_USA%29.JPG/500px-European_paintings_at_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_%28NYC%2C_USA%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:European_paintings_at_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_(NYC,_USA).JPG)

European paintings at the museum

In 2012 the Met's collection of European paintings numbered "more than 2,500 works of art from the thirteenth through the early twentieth century."[[32]](#cite_note-Metmuseum.org-32) As of December 2021, it had 2,625.[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33) These paintings are housed in the Old Masters galleries (newly installed in 2023),[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) the Nineteenth and Early Twentieth Century galleries reinstalled in 2007[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) (both on the second floor of the main building), the Robert Lehman Collection,[[38]](#cite_note-38) and the Jack and Belle Linsky Collection[[39]](#cite_note-39) (both on the first floor); a number of paintings also hang in other departmental galleries. Some of the medieval paintings are permanently exhibited at the Met Cloisters.[[32]](#cite_note-Metmuseum.org-32) The current curator in charge of the European Paintings department is Stephan Wolohojian.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### Old Master paintings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Old Master paintings")]

The collection began when 174 paintings were purchased from European dealers in 1871.[[32]](#cite_note-Metmuseum.org-32) Almost two-thirds of these paintings have been deaccessioned, but quality paintings by Jordaens, Van Dyck, Poussin, the Tiepolos, Guardi, and some other artists remain in the collection.[[41]](#cite_note-www.metmuseum.org-41) Major gifts from Henry Gurdon Marquand in 1889, 1890 and 1891[[42]](#cite_note-Quodbach-2017-42) gave the Met a much more solid foundation. Additionally, his example helped to create a taste for collecting Old Master paintings. In 1913, the Benjamin Altman bequest had sufficient range and depth to put the Met's collection of paintings on the map. In 1949, the Jules Bache gift added more great paintings.[[43]](#cite_note-Mayor-1957-43) The Robert Lehman Collection, which came to the museum in 1975, included many significant paintings, and is particularly strong in early Renaissance material. Over a period of decades, Charles and Jayne Wrightsman donated 94 works of unusually high quality to the Department of European Paintings, the last of which came with Mrs. Wrightsman's bequest in 2019. Notwithstanding the contributions made by Marquand, Altman, Bache, and Lehman, it has been written that "the Wrightsman paintings are highest in overall quality and condition."[[44]](#cite_note-Cordova-2020-44) The latter "collected expertise as well as art," and advanced technology made better choices possible.[[44]](#cite_note-Cordova-2020-44) Additionally, the Wrightsmans had the Met's curators at their disposal, for whom they served as a virtual "auxiliary purchase fund for objects the Met curators coveted, but could not afford."[[44]](#cite_note-Cordova-2020-44)

#### Plein Air, Impressionist, and Post-Impressionist Painting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Plein Air, Impressionist, and Post-Impressionist Painting")]

The Met's [plein air](/wiki/En_plein_air "En plein air") painting collection, which it calls "unrivaled",[[45]](#cite_note-Metmuseum.org-1-45) was the last large section of the European Paintings collection to have a home at the museum. The sale of a Monet and the construction of small scale galleries ultimately resulted in the acquisition of 220 European paintings (most of them plein-air sketches) from two collections. The Monet was used to purchase a half share of Wheelock "Lock" Whitney III's collection in 2003 (the remainder came as a promised gift), and when Eugene V. Thaw (1927–2018) saw how good they looked in the Met's new, purpose built galleries, he and his wife Clare donated their substantially larger collection to the Met (much of it a joint gift to the Morgan Library). The Met easily has the best collection of this material in the nation, and one of the three or four best in the world.[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33) Thus the Met's collection, hitherto top-heavy with famous French artists, "became uniquely diverse," with "many little-known artists from France, as well as numerous artists from other European nations;" many of which are not otherwise represented in U.S. museums.[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33) The plein-air collection forms a bridge "to what became the avant-garde," the Impressionists and their successors.[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33)

As noted by the museum, "a work by Renoir entered the Museum as early as 1907 (today the Museum has become one of the world's great repositories of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist art)."[[46]](#cite_note-46) The museum terms its nineteenth-century French paintings "second only to the museums of Paris," with strengths in "Gustave Courbet, Edgar Degas, Édouard Manet, Claude Monet, Paul Cézanne, Vincent van Gogh, and others."[[45]](#cite_note-Metmuseum.org-1-45)

The foundation of the museum's great Impressionist and Post-Impressionist collection was laid by the Louisine (1855–1929) and Henry Osborne Havemeyer (1847–1907) collection. The most important portion of their immense collection came to the museum after the death of Louisine in 1929.[[43]](#cite_note-Mayor-1957-43)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) It was particularly strong in works by Courbet, Corot, Manet, Monet, and, above all, Degas. The other remarkable gift of this material came from Walter H. and Leonore Annenberg, who, before they promised their collection to the Met in 1991, annually loaned it to the Met for half a year at a time.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) Walter Annenberg described his choice of gifting his collection to the Met as an example of "strength going to strength."[[51]](#cite_note-51) The two collections are highly complementary: "The Annenberg collection serves as a second, complementary core collection of blue chip Impressionist and Post-Impressionist paintings. Most importantly, it strengthened the Met's relatively sparse holdings of Gauguin and Toulouse-Lautrec, it added needed late works by Cézanne and Monet as well as a rare Seurat, and it brought a very impressive group of Van Goghs to a collection already rich in works by the Dutchman."[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33)

#### European sculpture and decorative arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: European sculpture and decorative arts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/NYC_-_Metropolitan_Museum_-_Carroll_and_Milton_Petrie_European_Sculpture_Court.jpg/250px-NYC_-_Metropolitan_Museum_-_Carroll_and_Milton_Petrie_European_Sculpture_Court.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYC_-_Metropolitan_Museum_-_Carroll_and_Milton_Petrie_European_Sculpture_Court.jpg)

European sculpture court

The European Sculpture and Decorative Arts collection is one of the largest departments at the Met, holding in excess of 50,000 separate pieces from the 15th through the early 20th centuries.[[45]](#cite_note-Metmuseum.org-1-45) Although the collection is particularly concentrated in [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") sculpture—much of which can be seen *[in situ](/wiki/In_situ "In situ")* surrounded by contemporary furnishings and decoration—it also contains comprehensive holdings of furniture, jewelry, glass and [ceramic pieces](/wiki/Ceramic_art "Ceramic art"), tapestries, textiles, and timepieces and [mathematical instruments](/wiki/Mathematical_instrument "Mathematical instrument"). In addition to its outstanding collections of English and French furniture, visitors can enter dozens of completely furnished period rooms, transplanted in their entirety into the Met's galleries. The collection even includes an entire 16th-century [patio](/wiki/Patio "Patio") from the Spanish castle of [Vélez Blanco](/wiki/V%C3%A9lez_Blanco "Vélez Blanco"), reconstructed in a two-story gallery, and the intarsia *[studiolo](/wiki/Studiolo "Studiolo")* from the ducal palace at [Gubbio](/wiki/Gubbio "Gubbio"). Sculptural highlights of the sprawling department include [Bernini](/wiki/Bernini "Bernini")'s *[Bacchanal](/wiki/A_Faun_Teased_by_Children "A Faun Teased by Children")*, a cast of [Rodin's](/wiki/Auguste_Rodin "Auguste Rodin") *[The Burghers of Calais](/wiki/The_Burghers_of_Calais "The Burghers of Calais")*, and several unique pieces by [Houdon](/wiki/Jean-Antoine_Houdon "Jean-Antoine Houdon"), including his *Bust of [Voltaire](/wiki/Voltaire "Voltaire")* and his famous portrait of his daughter Sabine.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

#### American Wing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: American Wing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_by_Emanuel_Leutze%2C_MMA-NYC%2C_1851.jpg/250px-Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_by_Emanuel_Leutze%2C_MMA-NYC%2C_1851.jpg)](/wiki/File:Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_by_Emanuel_Leutze,_MMA-NYC,_1851.jpg)

*[Washington Crossing the Delaware](/wiki/Washington_Crossing_the_Delaware_(1851_painting) "Washington Crossing the Delaware (1851 painting)")* by [Emanuel Leutze](/wiki/Emanuel_Leutze "Emanuel Leutze")

The museum's collection of American art returned to view in new galleries on January 16th, 2012. The new installation provides visitors with the history of [American art](/wiki/Visual_art_of_the_United_States "Visual art of the United States") from the 18th through the early 20th century. The new galleries encompasses 30,000 square feet (2,800 m2) for the display of the museum's collection.[[53]](#cite_note-53) The curator in charge of the American Wing since September 2014 is Sylvia Yount.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In July 2018, *Art of Native America* opened in the American Wing.[[56]](#cite_note-The_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art-56) This marked the first appearance of [Indigenous American art](/wiki/Visual_arts_of_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Visual arts of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas") in the museum's vast American wing.[[57]](#cite_note-57) *Art of Native America* was accompanied by a statement from the institution. "The American Wing acknowledges the sovereign Native American and Indigenous communities dispossessed from the lands and waters of this region. We affirm our intentions for ongoing relationships with contemporary Native American and Indigenous artists and the original communities whose ancestral and aesthetic items we care for."[[56]](#cite_note-The_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art-56) Contrary to this public statement, the museum came under immense scrutiny for the hazy provenance of the displayed items.[[58]](#cite_note-Sharp-2023-58) This was followed by the hiring of a new curator of Indigenous American art for the museum, [Dr. Patricia Marroquin Norby](/wiki/Patricia_Marroquin_Norby "Patricia Marroquin Norby"), who is of [Purépecha](/wiki/Pur%C3%A9pecha "Purépecha") descent.[[59]](#cite_note-Bahr-2020-59)

#### Greek and Roman art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Greek and Roman art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Photograph_of_the_New_Roman_Gallery_at_the_Metropolitan%E2%80%94New_York_City.jpg/250px-Photograph_of_the_New_Roman_Gallery_at_the_Metropolitan%E2%80%94New_York_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photograph_of_the_New_Roman_Gallery_at_the_Metropolitan%E2%80%94New_York_City.jpg)

Greek and Roman gallery

The Met's collection of [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_art "Ancient Greek art") and [Roman art](/wiki/Roman_art "Roman art") contains more than 17,000 objects.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The Greek and Roman collection dates back to the founding of the museum—in fact, the museum's first accessioned object was a Roman [sarcophagus](/wiki/Sarcophagus "Sarcophagus"), still currently on display.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Though the collection naturally concentrates on items from [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), these historical regions represent a wide range of cultures and artistic styles, from classic Greek [black-figure](/wiki/Black-figure_pottery "Black-figure pottery") and [red-figure](/wiki/Red-figure_pottery "Red-figure pottery") vases to carved Roman [tunic](/wiki/Tunic "Tunic") pins.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Limestone_sarcophagus-_the_Amathus_sarcophagus_MET_DT352.jpg/250px-Limestone_sarcophagus-_the_Amathus_sarcophagus_MET_DT352.jpg)](/wiki/File:Limestone_sarcophagus-_the_Amathus_sarcophagus_MET_DT352.jpg)

The [Amathus sarcophagus](/wiki/Amathus_sarcophagus "Amathus sarcophagus"), from [Amathus](/wiki/Amathus "Amathus"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), arguably the single most important object in the Cesnola Collection

Highlights of the collection include the monumental [Amathus sarcophagus](/wiki/Amathus_sarcophagus "Amathus sarcophagus") and a magnificently detailed [Etruscan](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization "Etruscan civilization") chariot known as the "[Monteleone chariot](/wiki/Monteleone_chariot "Monteleone chariot")". The collection also contains many pieces from far earlier than the Greek or Roman empires—among the most remarkable are a collection of early [Cycladic](/wiki/Cyclades "Cyclades") sculptures from the mid-third millennium BCE, many so abstract as to seem almost modern. The Greek and Roman galleries also contain several large classical wall paintings and reliefs from different periods, including an entire reconstructed bedroom from a noble [villa](/wiki/Villa "Villa") in [Boscoreale](/wiki/Boscoreale "Boscoreale"), excavated after its entombment by the eruption of [Vesuvius](/wiki/Mount_Vesuvius "Mount Vesuvius") in 79 [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era"). In 2007, the Met's Greek and Roman galleries were expanded to approximately 60,000 square feet (6,000 m2), allowing the majority of the collection to be on permanent display.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

#### Islamic art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Islamic art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Folio_Blue_Quran_Met_2004.88.jpg/250px-Folio_Blue_Quran_Met_2004.88.jpg)](/wiki/File:Folio_Blue_Quran_Met_2004.88.jpg)

Leaf from the [Blue Qur'an](/wiki/Blue_Qur%27an "Blue Qur'an") showing Chapter 30: 28–32

The Metropolitan Museum owns one of the world's largest collection of works of art of the Islamic world. The collection also includes artifacts and works of art of cultural and secular origin from the time period indicated by the rise of Islam predominantly from the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East") and in contrast to the [Ancient Near Eastern](/wiki/Ancient_Near_East "Ancient Near East") collections. The biggest number of [miniatures](/wiki/Miniature_(illuminated_manuscript) "Miniature (illuminated manuscript)") from the "[Shahnameh](/wiki/Shahnameh "Shahnameh")" list prepared under the reign of [Shah](/wiki/Shah "Shah") [Tahmasp I](/wiki/Tahmasp_I "Tahmasp I"), the most luxurious of all the existing [Islamic manuscripts](/wiki/Islamic_manuscripts "Islamic manuscripts"), also belongs to this museum. Other rarities include the works of [Sultan Muhammad](/wiki/Sultan_Mohammed "Sultan Mohammed") and his associates from the [Tabriz school](/wiki/Persian_miniature "Persian miniature") "The Sade Holiday", "Tahmiras kills divs", "[Bijan and Manijeh](/wiki/Bijan_and_Manijeh "Bijan and Manijeh")", and many others.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

The Met's collection of [Islamic art](/wiki/Islamic_art "Islamic art") is not confined strictly to [religious art](/wiki/Religious_art "Religious art"), though a significant number of the objects in the Islamic collection were originally created for religious use or as decorative elements in [mosques](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque"). Much of the 12,000 strong collection consists of secular items, including ceramics and [textiles](/wiki/Textiles "Textiles"), from Islamic cultures ranging from [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") to [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") to [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia").[[65]](#cite_note-65) The Islamic Art department's collection of miniature paintings from [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") and [Mughal India](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") are a highlight of the collection. [Calligraphy](/wiki/Calligraphy "Calligraphy") both religious and secular is well represented in the Islamic Art department, from the official decrees of [Suleiman the Magnificent](/wiki/Suleiman_the_Magnificent "Suleiman the Magnificent") to a number of [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran") manuscripts reflecting different periods and styles of calligraphy. Modern calligraphic artists also used a word or phrase to convey a direct message, or they created compositions from the shapes of Arabic words. Others incorporated indecipherable cursive writing within the body of the work to evoke the illusion of writing.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Islamic Arts galleries had been undergoing refurbishment since 2001 and reopened on November 1, 2011, as the New Galleries for the Art of the Arab Lands, Turkey, Iran, Central Asia, and Later South Asia. Until that time, a narrow selection of items from the collection had been on temporary display throughout the museum. As with many other departments at the Met, the Islamic Art galleries contain many interior pieces, including the entire reconstructed *Nur Al-Din Room* from an early 18th-century house in [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In September 2022 the Met revealed that it had received a substantial gift from [Qatar Museums](/wiki/Qatar_Museums "Qatar Museums") on the occasion of its 10th anniversary of the opening of its Galleries for the Art of the Arab Lands, Turkey, Iran, Central Asia and Later South Asia, which would benefit its Department of Islamic Art and some of the museum's other principal projects. As a token of its appreciation the name *Qatar Gallery* was adopted for the museum's Gallery of the [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") and [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate") Periods.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) This followed the announcement that the Met and Qatar Museums had entered into a partnership to foster their exchange with regards to exhibitions, activities, and scholarly cooperation.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### Non-geographically designated collections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Non-geographically designated collections")]

#### Arms and armor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Arms and armor")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Middle_Age_Main_Hall.jpg/250px-Middle_Age_Main_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Middle_Age_Main_Hall.jpg)

Arms and armor, Middle Ages main hall

The Met's Department of Arms and Armor is one of the museum's most popular collections.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Several early trustees of the museum were armor enthusiasts. The 1904 purchase of the collection of Maurice de Talleyrand-Périgord, duc de Dino, served as the foundational collection. It became a great collection with the gift and bequest of the Henry Riggs collection of 2,000 pieces, which was one of the finest assembled by a single person. It came to the museum in 1913 and 1925. Another collection landmark took place in 1936, when George Cameron Stone bequeathed 3,000 pieces of Asian armor. Bashford Dean, the first arms curator, did much to build up the collection, including with gifts he and his friends made directly to the Met, which enabled the purchase of his personal collection.[[43]](#cite_note-Mayor-1957-43)[[72]](#cite_note-Tomkins-1989-72)[[73]](#cite_note-www.metmuseum.org-2020-73)

Stephen V. Grancsay, the second arms curator at the museum, ably added to the collection, and he even purchased important works from Clarence H. Mackay (the greatest contemporary private collector of this material, who was wiped out by the Great Depression). Grancsay later resold some of these important works to the museum at cost.[[72]](#cite_note-Tomkins-1989-72)

The department's focus on "outstanding craftsmanship and decoration," including pieces intended solely for display, means that the collection is strongest in [late medieval](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages") [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") pieces and [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") pieces from the 5th through 19th centuries. However, these are not the only cultures represented in Arms and Armor; the collection spans more geographic regions than almost any other department, including weapons and armor from [dynastic Egypt](/wiki/Early_Dynastic_Period_of_Egypt "Early Dynastic Period of Egypt"), [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), the ancient [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East"), Africa, [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania"), and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), as well as American firearms (especially [Colt](/wiki/Colt%27s_Manufacturing_Company "Colt's Manufacturing Company") firearms) from the 19th and 20th centuries. Among the collection's 14,000 objects[[74]](#cite_note-74) are the oldest items in the museum: flint bifaces which date to 700,000–200,000 BCE. There are also many pieces made for and used by kings and princes, including armor belonging to [Henry VIII of England](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England"), [Henry II of France](/wiki/Henry_II_of_France "Henry II of France"), and [Ferdinand I, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Ferdinand_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand I, Holy Roman Emperor").[[75]](#cite_note-75) A. Hyatt Mayor called the Met's collection "the only single collection from which one might illustrate the whole history of the subject.[[43]](#cite_note-Mayor-1957-43)

The distinctive "parade" of armored figures on horseback installed in the first-floor Arms and Armor gallery is one of the most recognizable images of the museum, which was organized in 1975 with the help of the Russian immigrant and arms and armor scholar, Leonid Tarassuk (1925–90).

In 2020 the Met announced Ronald S. Lauder's promised gift of 91 objects from his collection, describing it as "the most significant grouping of European arms and armor given to the Museum since 1942," one that is "outstanding for the exceptional rarity and quality of the objects, their illustrious origins, and their typological variety."[[76]](#cite_note-76) Lauder, who noted that he had begun collecting with the assistance of curator Grancsay almost 55 years earlier, also donated money for the study and presentation of arms and armor. The 11 galleries were named in Lauder's honor.

#### Costume Institute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Costume Institute")]

Main article: [Anna Wintour Costume Center](/wiki/Anna_Wintour_Costume_Center "Anna Wintour Costume Center")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Robe_%C3%A0_la_Fran%C3%A7aise_MET_DT3884.jpg/250px-Robe_%C3%A0_la_Fran%C3%A7aise_MET_DT3884.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robe_%C3%A0_la_Fran%C3%A7aise_MET_DT3884.jpg)

Robe à la française 1740s, as seen in one of the exhibits at the Costume Institute

The Museum of Costume Art was founded by [Aline Bernstein](/wiki/Aline_Bernstein "Aline Bernstein") and [Irene Lewisohn](/wiki/Irene_Lewisohn "Irene Lewisohn").[[77]](#cite_note-77) In 1946, with the financial support of the fashion industry, the Museum of Costume Art merged with The Metropolitan Museum of Art as The Costume Institute, and in 1959 became a curatorial department.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Today, its collection contains more than 35,000 costumes and accessories.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The Costume Institute used to have a permanent gallery space in what was known as the "Basement" area of the Met because it was downstairs at the bottom of the Met facility. However, due to the fragile nature of the items in the collection, the Costume Institute does not maintain a permanent installation. Instead, every year it holds two separate shows in the Met's galleries using costumes from its collection, with each show centering on a specific designer or theme. The Costume Institute is known for hosting the annual [Met Gala](/wiki/Met_Gala "Met Gala") and in the past has presented summer exhibitions such as [Savage Beauty](/wiki/Savage_Beauty_(exhibition) "Savage Beauty (exhibition)") and [China: Through the Looking Glass](/wiki/China:_Through_the_Looking_Glass "China: Through the Looking Glass").[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

In past years, Costume Institute shows organized around designers such as [Cristóbal Balenciaga](/wiki/Crist%C3%B3bal_Balenciaga "Cristóbal Balenciaga"), [Chanel](/wiki/Chanel "Chanel"), [Yves Saint Laurent](/wiki/Yves_Saint_Laurent_(designer) "Yves Saint Laurent (designer)"), and [Gianni Versace](/wiki/Gianni_Versace "Gianni Versace"); and style doyenne like [Diana Vreeland](/wiki/Diana_Vreeland "Diana Vreeland"), [Mona von Bismarck](/wiki/Mona_von_Bismarck "Mona von Bismarck"), [Babe Paley](/wiki/Babe_Paley "Babe Paley"), [Jayne Wrightsman](/wiki/Jayne_Wrightsman "Jayne Wrightsman"), [Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis](/wiki/Jacqueline_Kennedy_Onassis "Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis"), [Nan Kempner](/wiki/Nan_Kempner "Nan Kempner"), and [Iris Apfel](/wiki/Iris_Apfel "Iris Apfel") have drawn significant crowds to the Met. The [Costume Institute's annual Benefit Gala](/wiki/Met_Ball "Met Ball"), co-chaired by *[Vogue](/wiki/Vogue_(magazine) "Vogue (magazine)")* editor-in-chief [Anna Wintour](/wiki/Anna_Wintour "Anna Wintour"), is an extremely popular, if exclusive, event in the fashion world; in 2007, the 700 available tickets started at $6,500 (~$9,446 in 2024) per person.[[83]](#cite_note-83)
Exhibits displayed over the past decade in the Costume Institute include: Rock Style, in 1999,[[84]](#cite_note-84) representing the style of more than 40 rock musicians, including [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna"), [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"), and [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"); Extreme Beauty: The Body Transformed, in 2001, which exposes the transforming ideas of physical beauty over time and the bodily contortion necessary to accommodate such ideals and fashion;[[85]](#cite_note-85) The [Chanel](/wiki/Chanel "Chanel") Exhibit, displayed in 2005,[[86]](#cite_note-86) acknowledging the skilled work of designer [Coco Chanel](/wiki/Coco_Chanel "Coco Chanel") as one of the leading fashion names in history; Superheroes: Fashion and Fantasy, exhibited in 2008,[[87]](#cite_note-87) suggesting the metaphorical vision of superheroes as ultimate fashion icons; the 2010 exhibit on the American Woman: Fashioning a National Identity, which exposes the revolutionary styles of the American woman from the years 1890 to 1940, and how such styles reflect the political and social sentiments of the time.[[88]](#cite_note-88) The theme of the 2011 event was "[Alexander McQueen](/wiki/Alexander_McQueen "Alexander McQueen"): [Savage Beauty](/wiki/Savage_Beauty_(exhibition) "Savage Beauty (exhibition)")".[[89]](#cite_note-89) Each of these exhibits explores fashion as a mirror of cultural values and offers a glimpse into historical styles, emphasizing their evolution into today's own fashion world. On January 14, 2014, the Met named the Costume Institute complex after [Anna Wintour](/wiki/Anna_Wintour "Anna Wintour").[[90]](#cite_note-90) The curator is [Andrew Bolton](/wiki/Andrew_Bolton_(curator) "Andrew Bolton (curator)").[[91]](#cite_note-91)

#### Drawings and prints

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Drawings and prints")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/D%C3%BCrer_Melancholia_I.jpg/250px-D%C3%BCrer_Melancholia_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:D%C3%BCrer_Melancholia_I.jpg)

*[Melencolia I](/wiki/Melencolia_I "Melencolia I")* by [Albrecht Dürer](/wiki/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer "Albrecht Dürer")

Though other departments contain significant numbers of [drawings](/wiki/Drawing "Drawing") and [prints](/wiki/Printmaking "Printmaking"), the Drawings and Prints department specifically concentrates on [North American](/wiki/North_America "North America") pieces and [Western European](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") works produced after the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). The first gift of Old Master drawings, comprising 670 sheets, was presented as a single group in 1880 by [Cornelius Vanderbilt II](/wiki/Cornelius_Vanderbilt_II "Cornelius Vanderbilt II"), though most proved to be misattributed.[[72]](#cite_note-Tomkins-1989-72) The Vanderbilt gift launched the collection, and the Department of Paintings also eventually acquired drawings (including by [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo") and [Leonardo](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci")). In the meantime, the Met library began to collect prints. Harris Brisbane Dick's donation of thirty-five hundred works on paper (mostly nineteenth-century etchings) and a fund for acquisitions led to the hiring of [William M. Ivins Jr](/wiki/William_Ivins_Jr. "William Ivins Jr."). in 1916.[[92]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Department-92)[[72]](#cite_note-Tomkins-1989-72)

As the museum's first curator of prints, Ivans established the mission of collecting images that would reveal "the whole gamut of human life and endeavor, from the most ephemeral of courtesies to the loftiest pictorial presentation of man's spiritual aspirations." Over the next 30 years, he built what is credited as the best collection in the nation.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Ivans opened three galleries and a study room in 1971. He curated almost sixty exhibitions, and his influential publications included *How Prints Look* (1943) and *Prints and Visual Communication* (1953), in addition to almost two hundred articles for the museum's *Bulletin*.[[73]](#cite_note-www.metmuseum.org-2020-73) Ivans and his successor [A. Hyatt Mayor](/wiki/A._Hyatt_Mayor "A. Hyatt Mayor") (hired 1932, 1946–66 Curator of Prints) collected hundreds of thousands of works, including photographs, books, architectural drawings, modern artworks on paper, posters, trade cards, and other ephemera.[[92]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Department-92) Important early donors to the department include: [Junius Spencer Morgan II](/wiki/Junius_Spencer_Morgan_II "Junius Spencer Morgan II"), who presented a broad range of material, mainly 16th century, including woodblocks and many prints by [Albrecht Dürer](/wiki/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer "Albrecht Dürer") in 1919; Gothic woodcuts and Rembrandt etchings from the [Felix M. Warburg](/wiki/Felix_M._Warburg "Felix M. Warburg") family; James Clark McGuire's transformative bequest brought over seven hundred fifteenth-century woodcuts; prints by Rembrandt, [Edgar Degas](/wiki/Edgar_Degas "Edgar Degas"), and [Mary Cassatt](/wiki/Mary_Cassatt "Mary Cassatt") with the [H.O. Havemeyer](/wiki/Henry_Osborne_Havemeyer "Henry Osborne Havemeyer") Collection in 1929. Ivans also purchased five albums from the auction of the Earl of Pembroke's collection, and the 2,200 prints in these albums provided a nucleus of Italian prints.[[73]](#cite_note-www.metmuseum.org-2020-73)[[92]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Department-92)

Meanwhile, acquisitions of drawings, including an album of 50 [Goyas](/wiki/Francisco_Goya "Francisco Goya") (thanks to Ivans, the Met collected almost 300 works by Goya on paper) continued to be processed through the Department of Paintings. In 1960, a Department of Drawings was established under Jacob Bean, who served as curator until 1992, during which time the museum's collection of drawings nearly doubled in size, with strengths in French and Italian works.[[73]](#cite_note-www.metmuseum.org-2020-73)[[94]](#cite_note-94)

Finally, in 1993, a unified Department of Drawings and Prints was created for all works on paper, chaired by [George Goldner](/wiki/George_R._Goldner "George R. Goldner"), who sought to rectify collecting imbalances by adding works by Dutch, Flemish, Central European, Danish, and British artists. The department has been led by [Nadine Orenstein](/wiki/Nadine_Orenstein "Nadine Orenstein"), Drue Heinz Curator in Charge since 2015. A particularly important recent gift was that of the Leslie and Johanna Garfield Collection of British Modernism in 2019.[[92]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Department-92)

The broadened collecting horizons of the museum in the post-Black Lives Matter era have been displayed in the exhibition of contemporary political works on paper called "Revolution, Resistance, and Activism", held at the Met in 2021–22. It included such works as the Guerrilla Girls' famous poster *Do women have to be naked to get into the Met. Museum?*, 1987, Julie Torres' *Super Diva!*, 2020 (a posthumous image of Supreme Court Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg), and Ben Blount's *Black Women's Wisdom*, 2019.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

Currently, the Drawings and Prints collection contains about 21,000 drawings, 1.2 million prints, and 12,000 illustrated books made in Europe and the Americas.[[96]](#cite_note-96) Many of the great masters of European painting, who produced many more sketches and drawings than actual paintings, are represented in the Drawing and Prints collection, sometimes in great concentrations. Prints are also represented in multiple states. Many artists and makers whose work is in the prints and drawings collection are otherwise not represented in the museum's holdings.

#### Robert Lehman Collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Robert Lehman Collection")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Robert_Lehman_Wing_-_Visitors_Watching_Impressionist_Masters.JPG/250px-Robert_Lehman_Wing_-_Visitors_Watching_Impressionist_Masters.JPG)](/wiki/File:Robert_Lehman_Wing_-_Visitors_Watching_Impressionist_Masters.JPG)

Robert Lehman Wing

On the death of banker [Robert Lehman](/wiki/Robert_Lehman "Robert Lehman") in 1969, his Foundation donated 2,600 works of art to the museum, which had been collected by Robert and his father.[[97]](#cite_note-97) Housed in the "Robert Lehman Wing", on the ground floor and the basement level, the museum refers to the collection as "one of the most extraordinary private art collections ever assembled in the United States".[[98]](#cite_note-98) To emphasize the personal nature of the Robert Lehman Collection, the Met housed the collection in a special set of galleries, some of which evoked the interior of Lehman's richly decorated [townhouse](/wiki/Townhouse "Townhouse") at [7 West 54th Street](/wiki/7_West_54th_Street "7 West 54th Street"). This intentional separation of the Collection as a "museum within the museum" met with mixed criticism and approval at the time, though the acquisition of the collection was seen as a coup for the Met.[[99]](#cite_note-Hoving-1993-99) Some have argued that it would be educationally more beneficial to have works from given schools of painting in the same section of the museum.

Unlike other departments at the Met, the Robert Lehman collection does not concentrate on a specific style or period of art; rather, it is a reflection of Lehman's personal collecting interests. The Lehmans concentrated heavily on paintings of the [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance"), particularly the [Sienese](/wiki/Siena "Siena") school. Sienese highlights include multiple major paintings by Ugolino da Siena, [Simone Martini](/wiki/Simone_Martini "Simone Martini"), [Sano di Pietro](/wiki/Sano_di_Pietro "Sano di Pietro"), and [Giovanni di Paolo](/wiki/Giovanni_di_Paolo "Giovanni di Paolo"), as well as a remarkable work by the [Osservanza Master](/wiki/Master_of_the_Osservanza_Triptych "Master of the Osservanza Triptych"). Other choice Italian paintings in the collection include masterpieces like [Botticelli](/wiki/Botticelli "Botticelli")'s *Annunciation*, a pair of stunning portraits by [Jacometto Veneziano](/wiki/Jacometto_Veneziano "Jacometto Veneziano"), and a stellar [Madonna and Child](/wiki/Madonna_and_Child "Madonna and Child") by [Giovanni Bellini](/wiki/Giovanni_Bellini "Giovanni Bellini").[[100]](#cite_note-100) The Northern school of painting is represented by [Petrus Christus](/wiki/Petrus_Christus "Petrus Christus"), [Hans Memling](/wiki/Hans_Memling "Hans Memling"), the Master of Moulins ([Jean Hey](/wiki/Jean_Hey "Jean Hey")), [Hans Holbein](/wiki/Hans_Holbein_the_Younger "Hans Holbein the Younger"), and [Lucas Cranach](/wiki/Lucas_Cranach_the_Elder "Lucas Cranach the Elder") and his studio. Dutch and Spanish Baroque highlights include the [Spanish](/wiki/Spain "Spain") painters [El Greco](/wiki/El_Greco "El Greco") and [Goya](/wiki/Francisco_Goya "Francisco Goya"), and the Dutch masters [Rembrandt](/wiki/Rembrandt "Rembrandt"), [Ter Borch](/wiki/Gerard_ter_Borch "Gerard ter Borch"), and de Hooch.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Lehman's collection of 700 drawings by the [Old Masters](/wiki/Old_Masters "Old Masters"), featuring works by [Rembrandt](/wiki/Rembrandt "Rembrandt") and [Dürer](/wiki/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer "Albrecht Dürer"), is particularly valuable for its breadth and quality. The collection also has French 18th and 19th century drawings,[[102]](#cite_note-102) as well as nearly two-hundred 18th century Venetian drawings, mostly by the Tiepolos.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

The collection of bronzes, furniture, Renaissance [majolica](/wiki/Maiolica "Maiolica"), [Venetian glass](/wiki/Venetian_glass "Venetian glass"), enamels, jewelry, textiles, and frames is outstanding.[[104]](#cite_note-104) The Lehman collection of Italian majolica is regarded as the best in the country.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

Robert Lehman also collected many nineteenth and twentieth century paintings. These include works by [Ingres](/wiki/Jean-Auguste-Dominique_Ingres "Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres"), [Corot](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste-Camille_Corot "Jean-Baptiste-Camille Corot"), the [Barbizon School](/wiki/Barbizon_School "Barbizon School"), [Monet](/wiki/Claude_Monet "Claude Monet"), [Renoir](/wiki/Pierre-Auguste_Renoir "Pierre-Auguste Renoir"), [Cezanne](/wiki/Paul_C%C3%A9zanne "Paul Cézanne"), [Gauguin](/wiki/Paul_Gauguin "Paul Gauguin"), [Van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh"), [Seurat](/wiki/Georges_Seurat "Georges Seurat"), and a number of Fauve painters, including [Matisse](/wiki/Henri_Matisse "Henri Matisse").[[106]](#cite_note-106) [Princeton University Press](/wiki/Princeton_University_Press "Princeton University Press") has documented the massive collection in a multi-volume book series published as *The Robert Lehman Collection Catalogues*.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

#### Medieval art and the Cloisters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Medieval art and the Cloisters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Pol%2C_Jean%2C_and_Herman_de_Limbourg.jpg/250px-Pol%2C_Jean%2C_and_Herman_de_Limbourg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pol,_Jean,_and_Herman_de_Limbourg.jpg)

The [Limbourg brothers](/wiki/Limbourg_brothers "Limbourg brothers")' *[Belles Heures of Jean de France, Duc de Berry](/wiki/Belles_Heures_of_Jean_de_France,_Duc_de_Berry "Belles Heures of Jean de France, Duc de Berry")*

The Met's collection of medieval art consists of a comprehensive range of Western art from the 4th through the early 16th centuries, as well as [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_art "Byzantine art") and pre-medieval European antiquities not included in the Ancient Greek and Roman collection. Like the Islamic collection, the Medieval collection contains a broad range of two- and three-dimensional art, with religious objects heavily represented. In total, the Medieval Art department's permanent collection numbers over 10,000 separate objects, divided between the main museum building on Fifth Avenue and [The Cloisters](/wiki/The_Cloisters "The Cloisters").[[108]](#cite_note-108)

##### Main building

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Main building")]

The medieval collection in the main Metropolitan building, centered on the first-floor medieval gallery, contains about 6,000 separate objects. While a great deal of European medieval art is on display in these galleries, most of the European pieces are concentrated at the Cloisters (see below). However, this allows the main galleries to display much of the Met's Byzantine art side by side with European pieces. The main gallery is host to a wide range of tapestries and church and funerary statuary, while side galleries display smaller works of precious metals and ivory, including [reliquary](/wiki/Reliquary "Reliquary") pieces and secular items. The main gallery, with its high arched ceiling, also serves double duty as the annual site of the Met's elaborately decorated Christmas tree.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

##### The Cloisters museum and gardens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: The Cloisters museum and gardens")]

Main article: [The Cloisters](/wiki/The_Cloisters "The Cloisters")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/The_Cloisters_Hudson_River_crop.jpg/330px-The_Cloisters_Hudson_River_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Cloisters_Hudson_River_crop.jpg)

[The Cloisters](/wiki/The_Cloisters "The Cloisters") from the Hudson River

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/The_Cloisters_-_The_MET_Cloisters_-_Joy_of_Museums_-_2.jpg/250px-The_Cloisters_-_The_MET_Cloisters_-_Joy_of_Museums_-_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Cloisters_-_The_MET_Cloisters_-_Joy_of_Museums_-_2.jpg)

Interior of the Cloisters

The Cloisters was a principal project of [John D. Rockefeller Jr.](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller_Jr. "John D. Rockefeller Jr."), a major benefactor of the Met. Located in [Fort Tryon Park](/wiki/Fort_Tryon_Park "Fort Tryon Park") and completed in 1938, it is a separate building dedicated solely to medieval art. The Cloisters collection was originally that of a separate museum, assembled by [George Grey Barnard](/wiki/George_Grey_Barnard "George Grey Barnard") and acquired *in toto* by Rockefeller in 1925 as a gift to the Met.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

The Cloisters are so named on account of the five medieval French [cloisters](/wiki/Cloisters "Cloisters") whose salvaged structures were incorporated into the modern building, and the five thousand objects at the Cloisters are strictly limited to medieval European works.[[111]](#cite_note-111) The collection features items of outstanding beauty and historical importance; among these are the *[Belles Heures of Jean de France, Duc de Berry](/wiki/Belles_Heures_of_Jean_de_France,_Duc_de_Berry "Belles Heures of Jean de France, Duc de Berry")* illustrated by the [Limbourg Brothers](/wiki/Limbourg_Brothers "Limbourg Brothers") in 1409,[[112]](#cite_note-112) the [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_art "Romanesque art") altar cross known as the "[Cloisters Cross](/wiki/Cloisters_Cross "Cloisters Cross")" or "Bury Cross",[[113]](#cite_note-113) and the seven [tapestries](/wiki/Tapestry "Tapestry") depicting the [Hunt of the Unicorn](/wiki/The_Hunt_of_the_Unicorn "The Hunt of the Unicorn").[[114]](#cite_note-114)

#### Modern and contemporary art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Modern and contemporary art")]

With some 13,000 artworks, primarily by European and American artists, the modern art collection occupies 60,000 square feet (6,000 m2), of gallery space[[115]](#cite_note-115) and contains many iconic modern works. Cornerstones of the collection include [Picasso](/wiki/Picasso "Picasso")'s portrait of [Gertrude Stein](/wiki/Gertrude_Stein "Gertrude Stein"), [Jasper Johns](/wiki/Jasper_Johns "Jasper Johns")'s *[White Flag](/wiki/White_Flag_(Johns_painting) "White Flag (Johns painting)")*, [Jackson Pollock](/wiki/Jackson_Pollock "Jackson Pollock")'s *[Autumn Rhythm (Number 30)](/wiki/Autumn_Rhythm_(Number_30) "Autumn Rhythm (Number 30)")*, and [Max Beckmann](/wiki/Max_Beckmann "Max Beckmann")'s [triptych](/wiki/Triptych "Triptych") *Beginning*. Certain artists are represented in remarkable depth, for a museum whose focus is not exclusively on modern art: for example, ninety works constitute the museum's Paul Klee collection, donated by [Heinz Berggruen](/wiki/Heinz_Berggruen "Heinz Berggruen"), spanning the entirety of the artist's life.[[116]](#cite_note-116) Due to the Met's long history, "contemporary" paintings acquired in years past have often migrated to other collections at the museum, particularly to the American and European Paintings departments.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

In April 2013, it was reported that the museum was to receive a collection worth $1 billion (~$1.32 billion in 2024) from cosmetics tycoon [Leonard Lauder](/wiki/Leonard_Lauder "Leonard Lauder"). The collection of [Cubist](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism") art includes work by Pablo Picasso, Georges Braque and Juan Gris and went on display in 2014.[[118]](#cite_note-118) The Met has since added to the collection, for example spending $31.8 million (~$39 million in 2024) for Gris' *The musician's table* in 2018.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

#### Musical instruments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Musical instruments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Grand_Pianoforte_MET_DP225545.jpg/250px-Grand_Pianoforte_MET_DP225545.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Pianoforte_MET_DP225545.jpg)

Grand piano by [Sébastien Érard](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_%C3%89rard "Sébastien Érard"), c. 1840

The Met's collection of musical instruments, with about 5,000 examples of musical instruments from all over the world, is virtually unique among major museums.[[120]](#cite_note-120) The collection began in 1889 with a donation of 270 instruments by [Mary Elizabeth Adams Brown](/wiki/Mary_Elizabeth_Adams_Brown "Mary Elizabeth Adams Brown"), who joined her collection to become the museum's first curator of musical instruments, named in honor of her husband, [John Crosby Brown](/wiki/John_Crosby_Brown "John Crosby Brown").[[121]](#cite_note-121) By the time she died, the collection had 3,600 instruments that she had donated and the collection was housed in five galleries. Instruments were (and continue to be) included in the collection not only on aesthetic grounds, but also insofar as they embodied technical and social aspects of their cultures of origin. The modern Musical Instruments collection is encyclopedic in scope; every continent is represented at virtually every stage of its musical life. Highlights of the department's collection include several [Stradivari](/wiki/Antonio_Stradivari "Antonio Stradivari") [violins](/wiki/Violin "Violin"), a collection of [Asian](/wiki/Asia "Asia") instruments made from precious metals, and the oldest surviving [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano"), a 1720 model by [Bartolomeo Cristofori](/wiki/Bartolomeo_Cristofori "Bartolomeo Cristofori"). Many of the instruments in the collection are playable, and the department encourages their use by holding concerts and demonstrations by guest musicians.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

#### Photographs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Photographs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/La_Tour_St._Jacques_La_Boucherie_%C3%A0_Paris_ca._1867.jpg/250px-La_Tour_St._Jacques_La_Boucherie_%C3%A0_Paris_ca._1867.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Tour_St._Jacques_La_Boucherie_%C3%A0_Paris_ca._1867.jpg)

*La Tour St. Jacques La Boucherie à Paris* by Charles Soulier, 1867

The Met's collection of [photographs](/wiki/Photograph "Photograph"), numbering more than 25,000 in total,[[123]](#cite_note-123) is centered on five major collections plus additional acquisitions by the museum. [Alfred Stieglitz](/wiki/Alfred_Stieglitz "Alfred Stieglitz"), a photographer himself, donated the first major collection of photographs to the museum,[[124]](#cite_note-124) which included a comprehensive survey of [Photo-Secessionist](/wiki/Pictorialism "Pictorialism") works, a rich set of master prints by [Edward Steichen](/wiki/Edward_Steichen "Edward Steichen"), and an outstanding collection of Stieglitz's photographs from his own studio. The Met supplemented Stieglitz's gift with the 8,500-piece [Gilman Paper Company Collection](/wiki/Gilman_Paper_Company_Collection "Gilman Paper Company Collection"), the Rubel Collection, and the Ford Motor Company Collection, which respectively provided the collection with early French and American photography, early British photography, and post-[WWI](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") American and European photography. The museum also acquired [Walker Evans](/wiki/Walker_Evans "Walker Evans")'s personal collection of photographs, a particular coup considering the high demand for his works.

The department of photography was founded in 1992. Though the department gained a permanent gallery in 1997, not all of the department's holdings are on display at any given time, due to the sensitive materials represented in the photography collection. However, the Photographs department has produced some of the best-received temporary exhibits in the Met's recent past, including a [Diane Arbus](/wiki/Diane_Arbus "Diane Arbus") retrospective and an extensive show devoted to spirit photography. In 2007, the museum designated a gallery exclusively for the exhibition of photographs made after 1960.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

#### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Film")]

The Met has an extensive archive consisting of 1,500 films made and collected by the museum since the 1920s. As part of the museum's 150 anniversary commemoration, since January 2020, the museum uploads a film from its archive weekly onto YouTube.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Themetcollection.svg/250px-Themetcollection.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Themetcollection.svg)

The Met collection

### Digital representation of collections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Digital representation of collections")]

Beginning in 2013, the Met organized the Digital Media Department for the purpose of increasing access of the museum's collections and resources using digital media and expanded website services. The first Chief Digital Officer [Sree Sreenivasan](/wiki/Sree_Sreenivasan "Sree Sreenivasan") from 2013 departed in 2016 and was replaced by Loic Tallon at the time that the department became known by its simplified designation as the Digital Department. At the start of 2017, the department began its Open Access initiative summarized on the Met's website titled "Digital Underground" stating: "It's been six months since the Met launched its Open Access initiative, which made available all 375,000+ images of public-domain works in the Met collection under Creative Commons Zero (CC0). During what is just the dawn of this new initiative, the responses so far have been incredible."[[127]](#cite_note-127) At that time, more than 375,000 photographic images from the museum's archival collection were released for public domain reproduction and use both by the general public and by large public access websites such as those available at Google BigQuery.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

In May 2022, the Met and the [World Monuments Fund](/wiki/World_Monuments_Fund "World Monuments Fund") announced a collaboration of digital work for the 2024 reopening of the African, ancient American, and Oceanic art galleries. The digital project "aims to bolster the understanding of several historic sites in sub-Saharan Africa", in particular sites that have been minimally explored by Western museums.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

Open access images and data have been viewed over 1.2 billion times with over 7 million downloads.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

## Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Libraries")]

Each curatorial department maintains a library, most of the material of which can be requested online through the libraries' catalog.[[131]](#cite_note-131) Two of the libraries may be accessed without an appointment:

### Thomas J. Watson Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Thomas J. Watson Library")]

Main article: [Thomas J. Watson Library](/wiki/Thomas_J._Watson_Library "Thomas J. Watson Library")

The [Thomas J. Watson Library](/wiki/Thomas_J._Watson_Library "Thomas J. Watson Library") is the central library of The Metropolitan Museum of Art, and supports the activities of staff and researchers. Watson Library's collection contains approximately 900,000 volumes, including monographs and exhibition catalogs; over 11,000 periodical titles; and more than 125,000 auction and sale catalogs.[[132]](#cite_note-132) The library includes a reference collection, auction and sale catalogs, a rare book collection, manuscript items, and vertical file collections. The library is accessible to anyone 18 years of age or older simply by registering online and providing a valid photo ID.[[133]](#cite_note-133)

### Nolen Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Nolen Library")]

The Nolen Library is open to the general public. The collection of some 8,000 items, arranged in open shelves, includes books, picture books, DVDs, and videos. The Nolen Library includes a children's reading room and materials for teachers.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

## Special exhibitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Special exhibitions")]

The museum regularly hosts notable special exhibitions, often focusing on the works of one artist that have been loaned out from a variety of other museums and sources for the duration of the exhibition. These exhibitions are part of the attraction that draw people both within and outside Manhattan to explore the Met. Such exhibitions include displays especially designed for the [Costume Institute](/wiki/Anna_Wintour_Costume_Center "Anna Wintour Costume Center"), paintings from artists from across the world, works of art related to specific art movements, and collections of historical artifacts. Exhibitions are commonly located within their specific departments, ranging from American decorative arts, arms and armor, drawings and prints, Egyptian art, Medieval art, musical instruments, and photographs. Typical exhibitions run for months at a time and are open to the general public. Each exhibition provides insight into the world of art as a transformative, cultural experience and often includes a historical analysis to demonstrate the profound impact that art has on society and its dramatic transformation over the years.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

In 1969, a special exhibition, titled "Harlem on My Mind" was criticized for failing to exhibit work by [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem") artists. The museum defended its decision to portray Harlem itself as a work of art.[[136]](#cite_note-136) [Norman Lewis](/wiki/Norman_Lewis_(artist) "Norman Lewis (artist)"), [Benny Andrews](/wiki/Benny_Andrews "Benny Andrews"), [Romare Bearden](/wiki/Romare_Bearden "Romare Bearden"), [Clifford Joseph](/w/index.php?title=Clifford_Joseph_(artist)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clifford Joseph (artist) (page does not exist)"), [Roy DeCarava](/wiki/Roy_DeCarava "Roy DeCarava"), [Reginald Gammon](/wiki/Reginald_Gammon_(American_artist) "Reginald Gammon (American artist)"), [Henri Ghent](/w/index.php?title=Henri_Ghent&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henri Ghent (page does not exist)"), [Raymond Saunders](/wiki/Raymond_Saunders_(artist) "Raymond Saunders (artist)"), and [Alice Neel](/wiki/Alice_Neel "Alice Neel") were among the artists who picketed the show.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

## Performance arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Performance arts")]

The museum's Department of Live Arts commissions new works of performance using music, sound, movement, and other artistic practices.[[138]](#cite_note-138) Under the banner of MetLiveArts, performances are presented in the Grace Rainey Rogers auditorium and the galleries of the [main museum building](/wiki/The_Met_Fifth_Avenue "The Met Fifth Avenue") and [The Cloisters](/wiki/The_Cloisters "The Cloisters"), and made available through digital platforms such as YouTube[[139]](#cite_note-139) and the museum's own website. Limor Tomer, became general manager of MetLiveArts in 2011,[[140]](#cite_note-140) moving the department once known as Concerts and Lectures away from the programs of classical recitals and chamber concerts that were customary at that time.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

Sarah Jones replaced Limor Tomer in 2025 as Head of Live Arts.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Metropolitan_opening_reception.jpg/250px-Metropolitan_opening_reception.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metropolitan_opening_reception.jpg)

Opening reception in the picture gallery at 681 Fifth Avenue, February 20, 1872; wood-engraving published in *[Frank Leslie's Weekly](/wiki/Frank_Leslie%27s_Weekly "Frank Leslie's Weekly")*, March 9, 1872

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: 19th century")]

The [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_Legislature "New York Legislature") granted the Metropolitan Museum of Art an Act of Incorporation on April 13, 1870, "for the purpose of establishing and maintaining in said City a Museum and Library of Art, of encouraging and developing the Study of the Fine Arts, and the application of Art to manufacture and natural life, of advancing the general knowledge of kindred subjects, and to that end of furnishing popular instruction and recreations".[[143]](#cite_note-143) This legislation was supplemented later by the 1893 Act, Chapter 476, which required that its collections "shall be kept open and accessible to the public free of all charge throughout the year".[[144]](#cite_note-nyt20170426-144) The founders included businessmen and financiers, among them [Theodore Roosevelt Sr.](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_Sr. "Theodore Roosevelt Sr."), the father of [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), the 26th president of the US, as well as leading artists and thinkers of the day, who wanted to open a museum to bring art and art education to the American people.[[4]](#cite_note-Met_History-4) Also instrumental in the founding of the museum was [Henry Gurdon Marquand](/wiki/Henry_Gurdon_Marquand "Henry Gurdon Marquand"), who donated an important part of his collection of Old Masters paintings to the fledgling institution.[[145]](#cite_note-libmma.org-2023-145) The [Marquand](/wiki/Marquand_and_Co. "Marquand and Co.") family, founders of [Marquand and Co.](/wiki/Marquand_and_Co. "Marquand and Co."), maintained a diverse interest in art based philanthropy, having donated large sums of money to Princeton University, as well as establishing [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Connecticut "Southport, Connecticut")'s [Pequot Library](/wiki/Pequot_Library "Pequot Library"), a special collections institution.[[145]](#cite_note-libmma.org-2023-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Henry_G._Marquand_MET_DT1512.jpg/250px-Henry_G._Marquand_MET_DT1512.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henry_G._Marquand_MET_DT1512.jpg)

The Metropolitan Museum of Art's second president, [Henry Gurdon Marquand](/wiki/Henry_Gurdon_Marquand "Henry Gurdon Marquand"). In 1896, the Museum trustees recognized Marquand's vast contributions to the institution by commissioning this portrait by [John Singer Sargent](/wiki/John_Singer_Sargent "John Singer Sargent").
