The museum first opened on February 20, 1872, housed in a building located at 681 Fifth Avenue.[[146]](#cite_note-146) [John Taylor Johnston](/wiki/John_Taylor_Johnston "John Taylor Johnston"), a railroad executive whose personal art collection seeded the museum, served as its first president, and the publisher [George Palmer Putnam](/wiki/George_Palmer_Putnam "George Palmer Putnam") came on board as its founding superintendent. The artist [Eastman Johnson](/wiki/Eastman_Johnson "Eastman Johnson") acted as co-founder of the museum,[[147]](#cite_note-147) as did landscape painter [Frederic Edwin Church](/wiki/Frederic_Edwin_Church "Frederic Edwin Church").[[148]](#cite_note-148) Various other industrialists, artists, and scientists of the age served as co-founders, including [Howard Potter](/wiki/Howard_Potter "Howard Potter"), [Salem Howe Wales](/wiki/Salem_Howe_Wales "Salem Howe Wales"), and [Henry Gurdon Marquand](/wiki/Henry_Gurdon_Marquand "Henry Gurdon Marquand"). Marquand's donated works are known as the [Marquand Collection](/wiki/Marquand_Collection "Marquand Collection").[[42]](#cite_note-Quodbach-2017-42) The former Civil War officer, [Luigi Palma di Cesnola](/wiki/Luigi_Palma_di_Cesnola "Luigi Palma di Cesnola"), was named as its first director. He served from 1879 to 1904. Under their guidance, the Met's holdings, initially consisting of a Roman stone sarcophagus and 174 mostly European paintings, quickly outgrew the available space. In 1873, occasioned by the Met's purchase of the [Cesnola Collection](/wiki/Luigi_Palma_di_Cesnola "Luigi Palma di Cesnola") of [Cypriot](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") antiquities, the museum decamped from Fifth Avenue and took up residence at the Mrs. Nicholas Cruger Mansion also known as the Douglas Mansion (James Renwick, 1853–54, demolished 1928) at 128 West 14th Street.[[149]](#cite_note-149) However, these new accommodations proved temporary, as the growing collection required more space than the mansion could provide.[[150]](#cite_note-Gross-2009-150) It moved into the current building in 1880. Between 1879 and 1895, the museum created and operated a series of educational programs, known as the [Metropolitan Museum of Art Schools](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_Schools "Metropolitan Museum of Art Schools"), intended to provide vocational training and classes on fine arts.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Metropolitan_Museum_circa_1914_LC-USZ62-101736.jpg/250px-Metropolitan_Museum_circa_1914_LC-USZ62-101736.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metropolitan_Museum_circa_1914_LC-USZ62-101736.jpg)

The museum in 1914

The museum is said to have established the world's first [museum shop](/wiki/Museum_shop "Museum shop") in 1908.[[152]](#cite_note-152) In 1954, to mark the opening of its Grace Rainey Rogers concert hall, the museum inaugurated a series of concerts, adding art lectures in 1956. This "Concerts & Lectures program" grew over the years into 200 events each season.[[153]](#cite_note-153) The program presented such performers as [Marian Anderson](/wiki/Marian_Anderson "Marian Anderson"), [Cecilia Bartoli](/wiki/Cecilia_Bartoli "Cecilia Bartoli"), [Judy Collins](/wiki/Judy_Collins "Judy Collins"), [Marilyn Horne](/wiki/Marilyn_Horne "Marilyn Horne"), [Burl Ives](/wiki/Burl_Ives "Burl Ives"), [Juilliard String Quartet](/wiki/Juilliard_String_Quartet "Juilliard String Quartet"), [Yo-Yo Ma](/wiki/Yo-Yo_Ma "Yo-Yo Ma"), [Itzhak Perlman](/wiki/Itzhak_Perlman "Itzhak Perlman"), [Artur Rubinstein](/wiki/Artur_Rubinstein "Artur Rubinstein"), [András Schiff](/wiki/Andr%C3%A1s_Schiff "András Schiff"), [Nina Simone](/wiki/Nina_Simone "Nina Simone"), [Joan Sutherland](/wiki/Joan_Sutherland "Joan Sutherland") and [André Watts](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Watts "André Watts"), as well as lectures on art history, music, dance, theater and social history. The program was directed, from its inception to 1968, by [William Kolodney](/wiki/William_Kolodney "William Kolodney"), and from 1969 to 2010, by Hilde Limondjian.[[154]](#cite_note-154)

In the 1960s, the governance of the Met was expanded to include, for the first time, a chairman of the board of trustees in contemplation of a large bequest from the estate of [Robert Lehman](/wiki/Robert_Lehman "Robert Lehman"). For six decades Lehman built upon an art collection begun by his father in 1911 and devoted a great deal of time to the Met, before finally becoming the first chairman of the board at the Metropolitan in the 1960s.[[155]](#cite_note-155) After his death in 1969, the Robert Lehman Foundation donated close to 3,000 works of art to the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Housed in the Robert Lehman Wing, which opened to the public in 1975 and largely financed by the Lehman Foundation, the museum has called it "one of the most extraordinary private art collections ever assembled in the United States".[[156]](#cite_note-156)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/20170721_Gotham_Shield_NYC_Aerials-225_medium.jpg/250px-20170721_Gotham_Shield_NYC_Aerials-225_medium.jpg)](/wiki/File:20170721_Gotham_Shield_NYC_Aerials-225_medium.jpg)

The museum (left foreground) is located in Central Park.

[The Metropolitan Museum of Art Centennial](/wiki/The_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_Centennial "The Metropolitan Museum of Art Centennial") was celebrated with exhibitions, symposia, concerts, lectures, the reopening of refurbished galleries, special tours, social events, and other programming for eighteen months from October 1969 through the spring of 1971. The centennial's events (including an open house, Centennial Ball, year-long art history course for the public, and various educational programming and traveling exhibitions) and publications drew on support from prominent New Yorkers, artists, writers, composers, interior designers, and art historians.[[157]](#cite_note-157)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In 2009 [Michael Gross](/wiki/Michael_Gross_(journalist) "Michael Gross (journalist)") published *The Secret History of the Moguls and the Money That Made the Metropolitan Museum, an unauthorized social history*,[[158]](#cite_note-158) and the museum bookstore declined to sell it.[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160)

In 2012, following the earlier appointment of [Daniel Brodsky](/wiki/Daniel_Brodsky "Daniel Brodsky") as chairman of the board at the Met, the by-laws of the museum were formally amended to recognize the office of the chairman as having authority over the assignment and review of both the offices of president and director of the museum.[[161]](#cite_note-NY_Times-2017-161) The office of chairman was first introduced relatively late in the museum's history in the 1960s in contemplation of the anticipated donation of the Lehman collection to the museum and has since that time, under Brodsky, become the most senior administrative position at the museum.[[161]](#cite_note-NY_Times-2017-161)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/MET_Breuer_%2848377070386%29.jpg/250px-MET_Breuer_%2848377070386%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:MET_Breuer_(48377070386).jpg)

The Met Breuer building in 2019

From 2016 to 2020, the museum operated a modern and contemporary art gallery at [945 Madison Avenue](/wiki/945_Madison_Avenue "945 Madison Avenue"), a [Marcel Breuer](/wiki/Marcel_Breuer "Marcel Breuer")-designed building at [Madison Avenue](/wiki/Madison_Avenue "Madison Avenue") and 75th Street in Manhattan's [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side"), the former [Whitney Museum of American Art](/wiki/Whitney_Museum_of_American_Art "Whitney Museum of American Art").[[162]](#cite_note-162) It extends the museum's modern and contemporary art program.[[163]](#cite_note-163) In September 2018, it was announced that the Met intended to vacate the Met Breuer three years early, in 2020; the [Frick Collection](/wiki/Frick_Collection "Frick Collection") began occupying the space while the main building underwent renovations.[[164]](#cite_note-164)[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166)

In January 2018, museum president Daniel Weiss announced that the century-old policy of free admission would be replaced by a $25 charge to out-of-state and foreign visitors, effective March 2018.[[167]](#cite_note-nypogrebin-167) The museum temporarily closed in March 2020 during the [COVID-19 pandemic in New York City](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_City "COVID-19 pandemic in New York City"), and reopened in late August;[[168]](#cite_note-168) this was the first time in over a century that the Met was closed for more than three consecutive days.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

In September 2020, following debates surrounding provenance holes and mismanagement of Indigenous art, the museum appointed Patricia Marroquin Norby ([Purépecha](/wiki/Pur%C3%A9pecha "Purépecha")/[Nde](/wiki/Apache "Apache") descent) as the museum's inaugural Associate Curator of Native American Art.[[59]](#cite_note-Bahr-2020-59)[[170]](#cite_note-170)[[171]](#cite_note-171) In May 2021, the museum installed a plaque on its Fifth Avenue facade in recognition of indigenous communities and of the fact that the museum is situated in what was historically [Lenapehoking](/wiki/Lenapehoking "Lenapehoking").[[172]](#cite_note-172)[[173]](#cite_note-173) That November, the Met received a $125 million donation from [Oscar L. Tang](/wiki/Oscar_Tang "Oscar Tang") and [Agnes Hsu-Tang](/wiki/Agnes_Hsu-Tang "Agnes Hsu-Tang"), the largest gift in the museum's history. In exchange, the Met named its modern and contemporary art galleries after the Tangs.[[174]](#cite_note-174) The following February, the Met hired [Moody Nolan](/wiki/Moody_Nolan "Moody Nolan") to renovate the Ancient Near Eastern and Cypriot galleries.[[175]](#cite_note-175) Mexican architect [Frida Escobedo](/wiki/Frida_Escobedo "Frida Escobedo") was hired in March 2022 to renovate the Tang Wing,[[176]](#cite_note-Dreith_2022_v150-176)[[177]](#cite_note-Niland_2013_a432-177) and the Met had raised $550 million for the Tang Wing by 2024.[[178]](#cite_note-Pogrebin_2024_g523-178) Designs for the Tang Wing were announced that December.[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180)

In 2024, several protests targeted the Met during the [Israel-Gaza war](/wiki/Israel-Gaza_war "Israel-Gaza war"), citing board members' investments in [RTX](/wiki/RTX_Corporation "RTX Corporation") and a lack of public comment around [destruction of cultural heritage during the war](/wiki/Destruction_of_cultural_heritage_during_the_Israeli_invasion_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Destruction of cultural heritage during the Israeli invasion of the Gaza Strip"). Met workers issued an open letter to the director calling for a public statement and to showcase [Palestinian art](/wiki/Palestinian_art "Palestinian art") held in storage. In a March 24 protest, advocates for the Palestinian cause and ceasefire laid a quilt on the front steps.[[181]](#cite_note-181)[[182]](#cite_note-182)

The Michael C. Rockefeller Wing closed temporarily in 2021 for a renovation designed by [Beyer Blinder Belle](/wiki/Beyer_Blinder_Belle "Beyer Blinder Belle") and WHY Architecture.[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184) The renovation was finished in May 2025.[[185]](#cite_note-185) In January 2026, the museum's workers voted to form a union affiliated with the [United Auto Workers](/wiki/United_Auto_Workers "United Auto Workers").[[186]](#cite_note-186)[[187]](#cite_note-187)

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Architecture")]

Main article: [The Met Fifth Avenue](/wiki/The_Met_Fifth_Avenue "The Met Fifth Avenue")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_-_panoramio_%284%29.jpg/250px-Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_-_panoramio_%284%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_-_panoramio_(4).jpg)

The Great Hall of the Met Fifth Avenue

After negotiations with the City of New York in 1871, the Met was granted the land between the East Park Drive, Fifth Avenue, and the 79th and [85th Street](/wiki/85th_Street "85th Street") transverse roads in [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"). The first part of the Met to be built was a red-brick and stone "mausoleum" designed by American architect [Calvert Vaux](/wiki/Calvert_Vaux "Calvert Vaux") and his collaborator [Jacob Wrey Mould](/wiki/Jacob_Wrey_Mould "Jacob Wrey Mould").[[188]](#cite_note-188) The Fifth Avenue facade, Great Hall, and Grand Stairway were designed in the [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") style by [Richard Morris Hunt](/wiki/Richard_Morris_Hunt "Richard Morris Hunt") and his son, [Richard Howland Hunt](/wiki/Richard_Howland_Hunt "Richard Howland Hunt"), in the late 1890s and early 1900s.[[189]](#cite_note-189) The firm of [McKim, Mead & White](/wiki/McKim,_Mead_%26_White "McKim, Mead & White") completed the wings on the Fifth Avenue facade in 1910. The modernistic glass sides and rear of the museum are the work of [Roche-Dinkeloo](/wiki/Roche-Dinkeloo "Roche-Dinkeloo").[[190]](#cite_note-190)

The Met Fifth Avenue measures almost 1⁄4-mile (400 m) long and with more than 2 million square feet (190,000 m2) of floor space, more than 20 times the size of the original 1880 building.[[191]](#cite_note-191)[[192]](#cite_note-192) The museum building is an accretion of over 20 structures, most of which are not visible from the exterior. The City of New York owns the museum building and contributes utilities, heat, and some of the cost of guardianship.[[193]](#cite_note-193)[[194]](#cite_note-194) The [Iris](/wiki/Iris_Cantor "Iris Cantor") and [B. Gerald Cantor](/wiki/B._Gerald_Cantor "B. Gerald Cantor") Roof Garden is located on the roof near the southwestern corner of the museum.[[195]](#cite_note-195)[[196]](#cite_note-196) The museum's main building was designated a city landmark by the [New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission](/wiki/New_York_City_Landmarks_Preservation_Commission "New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission") in 1967,[[197]](#cite_note-197) and its interior was separately recognized by the Landmarks Preservation Commission in 1977.[[198]](#cite_note-198) The Met's main building was designated a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") in 1986, recognizing both its monumental architecture, and its importance as a cultural institution.[[199]](#cite_note-199)

## Management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Management")]

### Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Governance")]

[Daniel Weiss](/wiki/Daniel_Weiss_(art_historian) "Daniel Weiss (art historian)") was the President and CEO of the Met, replacing Emily K. Rafferty, who served as president for a decade,[[200]](#cite_note-200) and [Thomas P. Campbell](/wiki/Thomas_P._Campbell "Thomas P. Campbell"), CEO and director of the museum until resigning in 2017. In April 2018, [Max Hollein](/wiki/Max_Hollein "Max Hollein") was named [director](/wiki/List_of_directors_of_the_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "List of directors of the Metropolitan Museum of Art").[[201]](#cite_note-201) The Met announced in August 2022 that Hollein became CEO in July 2023.[[202]](#cite_note-202)

#### Board

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Board")]

Although the City of New York owns the museum building and contributes utilities, heat, and some of the cost of guardianship, the collections are owned by a private corporation of fellows and benefactors which totals about 950 people. The museum is governed by a board of trustees of 41 elected members, several officials of the City of New York, and persons honored as trustees by the museum. The current co-chairs of the board, [Candace Beinecke](/wiki/Candace_Beinecke "Candace Beinecke") and [Hamilton E. James](/wiki/Hamilton_E._James "Hamilton E. James"), were elected in 2021.[[203]](#cite_note-203) Other notable trustees include [Anna Wintour](/wiki/Anna_Wintour "Anna Wintour"), [Richard Chilton](/wiki/Richard_Chilton "Richard Chilton"), [Candace Beinecke](/wiki/Hughes_Hubbard_%26_Reed "Hughes Hubbard & Reed"), [Alejandro Santo Domingo](/wiki/Alejandro_Santo_Domingo "Alejandro Santo Domingo").[[204]](#cite_note-The_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art-2014-204) The New York City Mayor, [City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council") speaker, and [New York City Comptroller](/wiki/New_York_City_Comptroller "New York City Comptroller") serve as [ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio "Ex officio") trustees, who appoint representatives to serve on the board without pay.[[205]](#cite_note-205)

The activities of board of trustees are organized and based upon the activities of the individual trustees and their various committees as of 2016.[[204]](#cite_note-The_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art-2014-204) The several committees of the board of trustees include the committees listed as Nominating, Executive, Acquisitions, Finance, Investment, Legal, Education, Audit, Employee Benefits, External Affairs, Merchandising, Membership, Building, Technology, and The Fund for the Met.[[204]](#cite_note-The_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art-2014-204)

### Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Finances")]

As of 2021, the museum's endowment as administered by the museum's investment officer Lauren Meserve[[206]](#cite_note-206) is US$3.3 billion which provides much of the income for operations while admissions account for only 13 percent of revenue as of fiscal 2016.[[207]](#cite_note-207)[[208]](#cite_note-208)[[209]](#cite_note-209) The 2009–10 operating budget was $221 million (~$313 million in 2024). The museum admission price as of March 2018 is $25 (~$32.00 in 2025) for out-of-state and foreign visitors, while New York state residents can [pay what they wish](/wiki/Pay_what_you_want "Pay what you want") to enter. Although subject to re-assessment,[[144]](#cite_note-nyt20170426-144) a 1970 agreement between the museum and the city of New York requires New York state visitors to pay at least a nominal amount; a penny is acceptable.[[210]](#cite_note-210) The Met's finance committee is led by [Hamilton E. James](/wiki/Hamilton_E._James "Hamilton E. James") of [The Blackstone Group](/wiki/The_Blackstone_Group "The Blackstone Group"), who is also one of the board members at the Met.[[211]](#cite_note-211) The Met is reported to have an Aaa [credit rating](/wiki/Bond_credit_rating "Bond credit rating"), the highest such rating possible. This was last affirmed by [Moody's](/wiki/Moody%27s "Moody's") in 2015.[[212]](#cite_note-212)

In 2019, museum president Daniel Weiss announced that the institution would review its policy for receiving financial donations, under pressure from activist group [P.A.I.N.](/wiki/P.A.I.N. "P.A.I.N.") for the role that cultural institutions have played in whitewashing the [Sackler family](/wiki/Sackler_family "Sackler family") by receiving their donations.[[213]](#cite_note-213) The museum announced it would remove the Sackler name from locations within the museum in December 2021.[[214]](#cite_note-214)

#### 2015–2018 setbacks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: 2015–2018 setbacks")]

In September 2016, *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* first reported financial set-backs at the museum related to servicing its outstanding debts and associated cut-backs in staffing at the museum, with the goal of trying to balance its budget by fiscal year 2018.[[215]](#cite_note-215) According to the Met's annual tax filing for fiscal year 2016, several top executives had received disproportionately high compensation, often exceeding $1 million per annum with over $100,000 bonuses per annum.[[216]](#cite_note-216)

In April 2017, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported that the Met's annual debt was approaching $40 million, in addition to an outstanding museum bond for $250 million. This resulted in the indefinite postponement of a planned $600 million architectural expansion of the exhibition space for the museum's modern art collection as well as started a general discussion over the Met's [human resources management](/wiki/Human_resources_management "Human resources management").[[217]](#cite_note-Pogrebin-2017a-217) The chairman of the board at the Met elected in 2011, [Daniel Brodsky](/wiki/Real_Estate_Board_of_New_York "Real Estate Board of New York"),[[218]](#cite_note-218) stated in response to the *Times* reports that he "looked forward to working with my administrative and board colleagues to support a climate of candor, transparency, accountability and mutual respect".[[217]](#cite_note-Pogrebin-2017a-217) In January 2018, Daniel Weiss as president of the museum stated that a downsized version of the original $600 million architectural expansion might be reconsidered as early as 2020 at a reduction to the $450 million level.[[167]](#cite_note-nypogrebin-167)

Brodsky, the chairman of the Met, stated that after the 2017 financial setbacks, the director position would be appointed separately from the position of CEO. Following a commissioned report from the [Boston Consulting Group](/wiki/Boston_Consulting_Group "Boston Consulting Group"), the interim CEO, president, and [COO](/wiki/Chief_Operating_Officer "Chief Operating Officer") of the Met, [Daniel Weiss](/wiki/Daniel_Weiss_(art_historian) "Daniel Weiss (art historian)"), said that the Met's 2015–2017 financial setbacks were caused by "slowing revenue, rising costs, and too many projects at once". Weiss was further reported as having hired Will Manzer, formerly an executive at [Perry Ellis](/wiki/Perry_Ellis_(brand) "Perry Ellis (brand)"), to help re-invigorate recently declining revenues at the museum.[[219]](#cite_note-219) On April 26, Weiss stated that the budget shortfall of $15 million might require a re-assessment and increase in the museum's current admission payment policy. Weiss added that there remained concerns for a sustainable fiscal model for the Met in which city officials "have a right to a clear understanding of how we would be engaging the public, how we balance access with sustainability".[[144]](#cite_note-nyt20170426-144) In May 2017, the Met filed formal proposal to attempt to charge admission fees to out-of-state visitors.[[220]](#cite_note-Pogrebin-2017b-220) [Robin Pogrebin](/wiki/Robin_Pogrebin "Robin Pogrebin"), writing for the *Times*, reported that the request for out-of-state admissions would call for the re-legislation of the New York State 1893 Act which requires that the museum's collections "shall be kept open and accessible to the public free of all charge throughout the year", and any unlegislated changes would be subject to challenge by the New York State attorney general, [Eric Schneiderman](/wiki/Eric_Schneiderman "Eric Schneiderman"), or one of the tristate counselors, [Christopher Porrino](/wiki/Christopher_Porrino "Christopher Porrino") or [George Jepsen](/wiki/George_Jepsen "George Jepsen").[[220]](#cite_note-Pogrebin-2017b-220)

In January 2018, Pogrebin writing for *The New York Times* reported that amid-continuing reverberations from "a period of financial turbulence and leadership turmoil" that the museum president Daniel Weiss had announced that the museum would rescind its century-old policy of free admission to the museum and begin charging $25 for out-of-state visitors starting in March 2018.[[167]](#cite_note-nypogrebin-167) Pogrebin stated that although the museum had made progress in decreasing its deficit from $40 million to $10 million, that an adverse decision from the City of New York to curtail funding for the Met's operating costs by as much as $8 million "for security and building staff" caused Weiss to announce the change in admissions policy. Weiss indicated that the new policy would be estimated to increase revenue from the current $43 million it receives from admissions to an enhanced revenue stream as high as US$49 million.[[167]](#cite_note-nypogrebin-167)

### Attendance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Attendance")]

For the fiscal year 2017 which ended on June 30, the museum was reported as having 7 million visitors during the past year, where "37 percent of these were international visitors, while 30 percent came from New York's five boroughs."[[221]](#cite_note-221) Previously in 2016, the museum set a record for attendance, attracting 6.7 million visitors—the highest number since the museum began tracking admissions.[[222]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-222) Forty percent of the Met's visitors in fiscal year 2016 came from New York City and the tristate area; 41 percent from 190 countries besides the United States.[[222]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-222) In 2017, the attendance figures indicated seven million annual visitors with 63% of the visitors arriving from outside of New York State.[[144]](#cite_note-nyt20170426-144)

[Roberta Smith](/wiki/Roberta_Smith "Roberta Smith") writing for *The New York Times* in September 2017 voiced growing public concern that proposed increases in admissions costs would have an adverse effect upon attendance statistics at the museum. Smith referred to the public perception that such costs would appear "greedy and inappropriate" because "The museum already gets around $39 million a year from its gate—equal to the entire annual budget of the [Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum")."[[223]](#cite_note-223) Smith's article continued to report the negative response of local communities in the tristate area surrounding the museum which was previously introduced in a series of articles by [Robin Pogrebin](/wiki/Robin_Pogrebin "Robin Pogrebin") written during the 2016–2017 fiscal year at the museum which criticized speculative suggestions among current administrators at the museum that an added revenue stream could be pursued by the museum by rescinding existing museum policy since 1893 allowing for free public access to the museum.[[144]](#cite_note-nyt20170426-144) In January 2018, museum president Daniel Weiss announced that the century-old policy of free museum admission would be replaced. Effective March 2018, most visitors who do not live in New York state or are not a student from New York, New Jersey, or Connecticut have to pay $25 (~$32.00 in 2025) to enter the museum.[[167]](#cite_note-nypogrebin-167) The City of New York has reduced funding at the Metropolitan as part of Mayor De Blasio's political effort to increase artistic diversity. They made an agreement to allow the fees in exchange for less funding which the city pledged to use at alternate facilities and promote diversity.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

Holland Carter and [Roberta Smith](/wiki/Roberta_Smith "Roberta Smith") of *The New York Times* argued in response to Weiss's decision to rescind the previous free admission policy as lacking in responsible fiscal planning. They stated that a recent $65 million expenditure for renovating fountains seemed to be a poor allocation of the limited available funding. Smith added, "Those new awful Darth Vaderish fountains take huge hunks out of the plaza and disrupt movement" as an indication of the misuse of funds.[[225]](#cite_note-225) Further criticism of Weiss's proposal was voiced internationally when *The Guardian* summarized the backlash from the Weiss proposal for raising the admissions fees. It stated, "Some critics are outraged. The past week has seen a *New York Times* piece titled "The New Pay Policy Is a Mistake", while Jezebel's Aimée Lutkin claimed "The Met Should Be Fucking Free". *The New York Post* writes that the museum has never had the right to charge admission and Alexandra Schwartz in the *New Yorker* says the new policy diminishes New York City".[[226]](#cite_note-226)

### Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic (2020–2021)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic (2020–2021)")]

In early 2020, the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") greatly impacted the Met's operations and led to the museum's first long-term shutdown on March 13. The Met gradually partially reopened in stages. By 2021, the public could visit the Met five days a week, with reduced hours of operation, and visitors were required to wear masks and practice [social distancing](/wiki/Social_distancing "Social distancing"). Several special exhibits were opened to the public during the reduced hours. There were 6,479,548 visitors in 2019, compared to 1,124,759 in 2020.[[227]](#cite_note-227) However, in 2021, the museum attracted 1,958,000 visitors, ranking fourth on the list of most-visited art museums in the world.[[228]](#cite_note-228)

Other services such as the research libraries were almost completely closed except for off-site digital access. As a result, 20 percent of staff positions were eliminated, and Met director Max Hollein indicated that the Met might deaccession and sell off some of its collection to make up financial shortfalls. At least some of the museum's large art holding was placed in storage in order to make-up for losses in revenue causes by responses to the pandemic.[[229]](#cite_note-229)

## Deaccessioning works of art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Deaccessioning works of art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Retrato_de_Juan_Pareja%2C_by_Diego_Vel%C3%A1zquez.jpg/250px-Retrato_de_Juan_Pareja%2C_by_Diego_Vel%C3%A1zquez.jpg)](/wiki/File:Retrato_de_Juan_Pareja,_by_Diego_Vel%C3%A1zquez.jpg)

[Diego Velázquez](/wiki/Diego_Vel%C3%A1zquez "Diego Velázquez"), *[Portrait of Juan de Pareja](/wiki/Portrait_of_Juan_de_Pareja "Portrait of Juan de Pareja")*, 1650

The Met has always [deaccessioned](/wiki/Deaccessioned "Deaccessioned") works to improve the collection by using the proceeds to purchase better or more appropriate works. As noted above, it has deaccessioned nearly two-thirds of the founding purchase of Old Master paintings made in 1871.[[41]](#cite_note-www.metmuseum.org-41) As early as 1887, it sold 5,000 Cypriot objects in order to purchase Egyptian antiquities, and over the years it sold many thousands more works from Cyprus.[[150]](#cite_note-Gross-2009-150) 15,000 Egyptian objects were sold in the museum's shop in 1955, and nearly 10,000 works from other departments were earmarked that year for sale at auction.[[150]](#cite_note-Gross-2009-150)

The Metropolitan Museum of Art spent $39 million to acquire art for the fiscal year ending in June 2012.[[230]](#cite_note-230) In 2020, it spent $29,824,000 (with $6,747,000 coming from insurance and the sale of art).[[231]](#cite_note-231) In 2021, it spent $36,402,000 (with $4,007,000 coming from insurance and the sale of art).[[232]](#cite_note-232) In 2022, it spent $74,432,000 (with $9,488,000 coming from insurance and the sale of art); in 2023 it spent $52,401,000 (with $7,444,000 coming from insurance and the sale of art).[[233]](#cite_note-233)

In the early 1970s, under the directorship of [Thomas Hoving](/wiki/Thomas_Hoving "Thomas Hoving"), the Met revised its deaccessioning policy. It sought to acquire "world-class" pieces, including through the sale of mid- to high-value items from its collection.[[99]](#cite_note-Hoving-1993-99) Though the Met had always sold duplicate or minor items from its collection to fund the acquisition of new pieces, the Met's new policy allowed the sale of important items with high values, objects regarded as part of the museum's core collection. Hoving's deaccessioning practices, including secretive non-public sales that violated donor wishes, was exposed by [John L. Hess](/wiki/John_L._Hess "John L. Hess") in a series of articles in the *New York Times*. These exposés provoked widespread criticism when they came to light, and they were compounded by deceitful and misleading statements made by Hoving, who was censured by the College Art Association and the Art Dealers Association of America.[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33) This resulted in an investigation of the museum by the [New York State Attorney General](/wiki/New_York_State_Attorney_General "New York State Attorney General") [Louis J. Lefkowitz](/wiki/Louis_J._Lefkowitz "Louis J. Lefkowitz"), who held public hearings in 1972. As a result of these hearings, the museum agreed to list in its annual report the total cash proceeds from art sales each year, and to itemize any deaccessioned objects valued at more than $50,000 each. It also agreed to sell those pieces at public auction and provide advance public notice of a work being sold if it had been on view in the last ten years.[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33)[[234]](#cite_note-234) Known as the "Hoving Affair", the deaccessioning scandal has been written about by [Calvin Tomkins](/wiki/Calvin_Tomkins "Calvin Tomkins"), in *Merchants and Masterpieces* (1970; rev. ed. 1989), [Michael Gross](/wiki/Michael_Gross_(journalist) "Michael Gross (journalist)") in *Rogues' Gallery* (2009), and Martin Gammon, *Deaccessioning and its Discontents: A Critical History* (2018),[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33) though the most comprehensive account remains Hess's 1974 book, *The Grand Acquisitors*.[[235]](#cite_note-235)

Two of the objects purchased with funds generated by Hoving's deaccessions were highlights of the Met's collection. [Diego Velázquez](/wiki/Diego_Vel%C3%A1zquez "Diego Velázquez")'s 1650 *[Portrait of Juan de Pareja](/wiki/Portrait_of_Juan_de_Pareja "Portrait of Juan de Pareja")* (bought in part through deaccessioned works) and a classical Greek vase, the [Euphronios Krater](/wiki/Euphronios_Krater "Euphronios Krater"), which depicted the death of [Sarpedon](/wiki/Sarpedon_(Trojan_War_hero) "Sarpedon (Trojan War hero)") (funded by the sale of the museum's classical coin collection). The latter, which proved to be looted, was repatriated to Italy in 2006. The Met has sold such valuable pieces as [Edward Steichen](/wiki/Edward_Steichen "Edward Steichen")'s 1904 photograph *[The Pond-Moonlight](/wiki/The_Pond-Moonlight "The Pond-Moonlight")* (which it regarded as a duplicate, since another copy was already in the Met's collection) for a record price of $2.9 million.[[236]](#cite_note-236)

Hoving was criticized for selling important works from the museum to fund his acquisitions, including a Henri Rousseau and a Van Gogh, and he planned to sell many more, including 14 Monet paintings he characterized as "routine".[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33) During the tenure of director Philippe de Montebello, the sale of a single Monet (together with the construction of purpose-built galleries) eventually led to the acquisition of two collections totaling 220 paintings, which established the museum's remarkable plein-air paintings collection.[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33)

Another deaccessioning controversy broke out in 2021, when the Association of Art Museum Directors temporarily relaxed its guidelines due to hardships suffered by museums during the COVID pandemic. Previously, funds from deaccessioned works were only to be used to purchase other works for the permanent collection. The temporary guidelines, however, permitted these monies to be used for the "care" of the collection. The Met decided to use funds from deaccessions for collection care (to pay salaries). It was roundly criticized for this decision by the Met's former director Thomas P. Campbell (Montebello's successor), by cultural critic Lee Rosenbaum, and by Pulitzer Prize winning journalist Christopher Knight, among others. They argued that the practice set a bad example for other museums and that the Met did not truly need these monies.[[33]](#cite_note-Cordova-2021-33)

## Looted art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Looted art")]

See also: [Looted art](/wiki/Looted_art "Looted art")

One of the most serious and daunting challenges to the Metropolitan Museum's respectable reputation has been a series of allegations and lawsuits about its known status as an institutional buyer of looted and stolen antiquities. Since the 1990s the Met has been the subject of countless investigative reports and books critical of the Met's *laissez-faire* attitude to acquisition.[[237]](#cite_note-IllicitTombRaiders-237)[[238]](#cite_note-238) The Met has lost several major lawsuits, notably against the governments of Italy and Turkey, which successfully sought the repatriation of hundreds of ancient Mediterranean and Middle Eastern antiquities, with a total value in the hundreds of millions of dollars.[[237]](#cite_note-IllicitTombRaiders-237) In August 2022, it was reported that the Cambodian government was pressuring the museum to return Khmer artifacts that were allegedly looted during the civil war and the tumultuous period following.[[239]](#cite_note-239) In September 2022, New York law enforcement, across three separate search warrants, seized 27 artifacts highlighting ancient Rome, Greece, and Egypt, with the intention of returning them to Italy and Egypt.[[240]](#cite_note-Mashberg-2022-240)[[241]](#cite_note-Jernudd-2023-241) These 27 objects were worth an aggregate total of $13 million.[[240]](#cite_note-Mashberg-2022-240) The items were seized due to their discovered link to known antiquities traffickers.[[241]](#cite_note-Jernudd-2023-241) This comes along with an international recalculation of what belongs in the museums of the world.[[240]](#cite_note-Mashberg-2022-240) Similar cases of repatriation conversations include the [Elgin Marbles](/wiki/Elgin_Marbles "Elgin Marbles"), the [Benin Bronzes](/wiki/Benin_Bronzes "Benin Bronzes"), and [El Penacho](/wiki/Montezuma%27s_headdress "Montezuma's headdress"). Along with the conversation about returning art, the art community is calling upon museological institutions to hold themselves to a higher standard of Social Justice, equity, and ethics.[[242]](#cite_note-242) On March 22, 2023, Manhattan prosecutors seized 15 allegedly stolen antiquities tied to [Subhash Kapoor](/wiki/Subhash_Kapoor_(art_dealer) "Subhash Kapoor (art dealer)").[[243]](#cite_note-243) In December 2023, the museum announced it will return 14 Khmer sculptures to Cambodia and 2 to Thailand after determining they were stolen and linked to art dealer [Douglas Latchford](/wiki/Douglas_Latchford "Douglas Latchford").[[244]](#cite_note-244)

### Looted Indigenous American art and NAGPRA violations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Looted Indigenous American art and NAGPRA violations")]

See also: [Visual arts of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Visual_arts_of_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Visual arts of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas")

Since 1993, Charles and Valerie Diker have donated 139 Indigenous objects to the museum, many of which are funerary. Most of these objects have [ownership histories](/wiki/Provenance "Provenance") with 200–2,000 year gaps in known owners.[[58]](#cite_note-Sharp-2023-58) The display of Native American funerary objects is problematic, not just because of its unethical spotlighting of private ceremonial objects, but also due to its violation of [NAGPRA](/wiki/Native_American_Graves_Protection_and_Repatriation_Act "Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act"). NAGPRA, the Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act, established in 1990, states that institutions that receive federal funding must work to [repatriate](/wiki/Repatriation "Repatriation") Native American [human remains](/wiki/Human_remains_(archaeology) "Human remains (archaeology)"), [funerary objects](/wiki/Grave_goods "Grave goods"), and other [ceremonially important objects](/w/index.php?title=Ceremonial_Objects&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ceremonial Objects (page does not exist)").[[245]](#cite_note-www.nps.gov-245) This was done to ensure that the respect and dignity deserved by all humans was given to Native Americans. Part of the goal of enacting NAGPRA was to begin a dialogue between American museum institutions and Indigenous groups. Written into NAGPRA is an acknowledgement of museums as the preservers of history.[[245]](#cite_note-www.nps.gov-245) The Met has been able to avoid the repatriation of valuable objects that fall under the grasp of NAGPRA because they have not formally owned them. Objects that are on permanent or semi-permanent loan to a museum do not legally necessitate the initiation of the repatriation process.[[58]](#cite_note-Sharp-2023-58) As the Diker collection is slowly incorporated into the Met's permanent collection, the objects will become illegal possessions, as the Met is a publicly funded institution.[[246]](#cite_note-Artforum-2023-246)

In April 2023, [ProPublica](/wiki/ProPublica "ProPublica") published a report[[247]](#cite_note-247) detailing the Indigenous American collections of the Met Museum. The report exposed the loophole of loan vs. own that the Met was using to cling onto objects that they had an ethical and legal responsibility to repatriate. The report came to the conclusion that the Native American art held by the Met could only have come into the possession of the Diker's through violence, [looting](/wiki/Looting "Looting"), and [dispossession](/wiki/Dispossession "Dispossession"). Additionally, the report exposed that only 15% of the 193 objects donated to the Met by the Diker collection have known provenance.[[58]](#cite_note-Sharp-2023-58) The Report was the cause of international outcry due to its bold pinpointing of just some of the Met's shoddy [acquisitioning](/w/index.php?title=Art_Acquisition&action=edit&redlink=1 "Art Acquisition (page does not exist)") history.[[248]](#cite_note-The_Art_Newspaper-2018-248)[[246]](#cite_note-Artforum-2023-246) This callout of the unequivocally respected institution that is the Met Museum caused a reckoning amongst museums displaying Indigenous American art.[[249]](#cite_note-249) In response to this scandal, the Met claimed that they were in contact with tribal governments about creating appropriate displays of Native American art and funerary objects, but did not specify which tribes.[[248]](#cite_note-The_Art_Newspaper-2018-248)

### Head of a Griffin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Head of a Griffin")]

In February 2025, the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York announced the return of a 2,700-year-old bronze griffin head to Greece. The artifact, dating to the 7th century B.C., had been in the museum's collection since 1950. An internal investigation, prompted by new information from Greek authorities and independent researchers, confirmed that the piece had been looted from an archaeological site before reaching the museum through a private collector.[[250]](#cite_note-250)

#### Collecting practices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Collecting practices")]

In response to many controversies, the museum issued a statement on collecting practices. The statement encompasses all 1.5 million works of art held by the Met.[[251]](#cite_note-251) Referencing research, transparency, and collaboration, this statement is a clear redefining of the Met's outlook on looted art and artwork with unknown histories. "As a pre-eminent voice in the global art community, it is incumbent upon the Met to engage more intensively and proactively in examining certain areas of our collection," stated Max Hollein, the director of the museum.[[252]](#cite_note-252) The Met hired a manager of provenance research with a team of three staff to assist the already-employed curators and historians.
